note: Add new grants here. Dates are ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). Set deadline to null for rolling/undated. Set addedDate to the date you added the entry (it controls RSS ordering and the 'new' badge). Categorisation in the page is automatic based on deadline. Field 'applicant' is one of 'individuals', 'organizations' or 'both' and drives the Who Can Apply filter; existing entries were seeded by a keyword heuristic that defaults to 'individuals' when ambiguous (this site is individual-focused), so correct it by hand when you know better. Optional field: 'suggestedBy' (string, e.g. '@handle' or a name) renders a small 'Suggested by ...' credit line on the card; only set this if the submitter opted in via the Suggest a grant form. Optional field: 'feed' set to false keeps the entry out of ALL RSS feeds while still showing it on the website, static SEO pages and calendars.

grants.title: imprint: a QTBIPOC Dance Writing Fellowship
grants.location: San Francisco Bay Area, California, United States
grants.amount: USD 500 stipend per fellow, plus writing and publishing mentorship
grants.duration: One program year (approximately September to September)
grants.description: imprint is an annual dance writing fellowship from pateldanceworks for emerging QTBIPOC (queer, trans, Black, Indigenous, and people of color) dance writers based in the San Francisco Bay Area. Artistic Director Bhumi B Patel supports three selected fellows with writing and publishing mentorship and professional development over a program year. Each fellow receives a USD 500 stipend and commits roughly 30 hours across six cohort meetings that include readings, discussions, writing feedback, and guest speakers. Fellows attend at least five Bay Area dance performances and have two to three completed pieces published on the pateldanceworks blog. The program is not intended for full-time students given the time requirements. There is no application fee.

grants.title: PLAYA Art/Sci Awarded Residency 2027
grants.location: Summer Lake, Lake County, Oregon, USA
grants.amount: Awarded residency providing free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal. Residents cover their own travel, groceries, and materials. No cash stipend. USD 40 application fee (waivers granted on request by June 28, 2026)
grants.description: PLAYA's Art/Sci Awarded Residency brings together artists and scientists for immersive, interdisciplinary inquiry at a rural retreat near Summer Lake in Lake County, Oregon. The 2027 cycle offers four session options ranging from 11 to 25 nights between January and November 2027. Awarded residents receive free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal, while covering their own travel, food, and materials. Both established and emerging practitioners in any media may apply, including naturalists, biologists, sound artists, new media artists, writers, performers, and interdisciplinary creators, and all applicants must be at least 18 years old. Applications are submitted through SlideRoom and require work samples and two references. There is a USD 40 application fee, though PLAYA states all fee waiver requests will be granted if submitted by June 28, 2026.

grants.title: Art Omi: Artists Residencies
grants.location: Ghent, New York, USA (Hudson Valley)
grants.amount: No cost to attend (lodging, dedicated workspace, and prepared meals provided); residents cover their own travel. No stipend.
grants.duration: About 2.5 weeks for most programs; Writers and Translation sessions run roughly 27 days
grants.description: Art Omi runs five residency programs on its 120-acre campus in Ghent, New York, in the Hudson Valley, about two and a half hours north of New York City. The programs cover visual art (Artists), architecture, dance, music, and writers and translation, selecting participants through a competitive jury process. Selected residents attend at no cost except travel, receiving comfortable lodgings, dedicated workspaces, and fresh prepared meals. Programs are open to international applicants and there is no fee to apply. The visual art, architecture, music, and dance cycles share one annual deadline (most recently October 15), while the Writers and Translation Lab has its own deadline; the next open cycle is the Writers and Translation Lab 2027, due July 1, 2026.

grants.title: Yaddo Residency
grants.location: Saratoga Springs, New York, USA
grants.amount: Room, board, and studio space provided at no cost; no stipend USD 35 application fee
grants.description: Yaddo is a retreat for artists on a 400-acre estate in Saratoga Springs, New York, where residents can work without interruption in a supportive environment. Residencies last from two weeks to two months and include a private bedroom, board with communal dinners and packed lunches, and an assigned studio or workspace. Applications are accepted from individual artists working in choreography, film, literature, musical composition, painting, performance, photography, printmaking, sculpture, and video. Applicants must be working at a professional level or be emerging artists whose work shows great professional promise, and they may not be enrolled in degree programs. International artists are welcome. There are two cycles each year, with the summer deadline on July 1 for residencies running November 2026 through June 2027.

grants.title: Wassaic Project 2027 Residency Open Call
grants.location: Wassaic, New York, USA
grants.amount: Studio space and a private bedroom in a shared house provided; the organization covers approximately USD 5,000 per person through donor and grant support. Residency fee on a sliding scale of USD 0 to 900 per session. USD 25 application fee (waivers available for financial hardship)
grants.duration: Sessions range from roughly 2 to 4 weeks within the Winter (Jan-April 2027) or Summer (June-Sept 2027) programs
grants.description: The Wassaic Project runs a single 2027 Artist Residency open call covering both the Winter Residency (January to April 2027) and the Summer Residency (June to September 2027) in Wassaic, New York. Residents receive a private studio (about 100 square feet in winter, 200 to 300 square feet in summer), a private bedroom in a shared house with common spaces and kitchen, and access to a wood shop in both seasons and a print shop in summer. Programming includes open studios, group studio visits, and individual artist talks. The residency operates on a sliding scale fee of USD 0 to 900 per session, with the organization covering roughly USD 5,000 per person through donor and grant support. The open call is open to individual artists, collaborative teams, groups of two or more, and applicants through the Family Residency program. This is the residency program, distinct from the separate Summer Exhibition open call.

grants.title: CIRCLE Expanded Orbit 1st Edition
grants.location: Athens, Greece
grants.amount: No cash stipend. Provides individual mentorship from world-leading experts, group sessions, co-creation workshops, structured feedback, and access to an international professional network across film, art, design, and technology. Participation fee of EUR 2,000 per person (EUR 4,000 per team of two), with scholarships covering up to 50 percent available for those with financial need. EUR 2,000 participation fee per person (EUR 4,000 per team of two); up to 50 percent scholarships available
grants.description: CIRCLE Expanded Orbit is the first edition of an international training programme dedicated to immersive storytelling and XR-based practices, run by the CIRCLE Training Initiative in partnership with Onassis ONX. It supports women and gender-expansive filmmakers, artists, XR creators, and multidisciplinary storytellers working across XR, MR, VR, AR, AI, and game-based narratives, with no prior XR experience required. Selected individuals or teams of up to two receive individual mentorship, group sessions, co-creation workshops, and structured feedback, plus access to an international network of professionals. The in-person training takes place from November 21 to 26, 2026, in Athens, Greece. Applicants should be developing a project with a strong artistic vision and clear interest in immersive formats, ideally with a producer attached and partial budget secured. A participation fee of EUR 2,000 per person applies, with scholarships of up to 50 percent available based on financial need.

grants.title: CIRCLE Fiction Orbit 3rd Edition
grants.location: Herceg Novi, Montenegro
grants.amount: Accommodation and meals provided; in-depth script consultations, legal and production strategy guidance, career development mentorship, and psychological support. Travel not covered. Participation fee of EUR 2,000 per person applies. At least one 50 percent scholarship available annually for participants from lower-income countries, those without national funding, or with demonstrated financial need. EUR 2,000 participation fee (accommodation and meals included; travel not covered); 50 percent scholarship available for demonstrated need
grants.description: CIRCLE Fiction Orbit is a one-week retreat in Herceg Novi, Montenegro for up to six fiction feature film projects led by teams of women and gender-expansive filmmakers developing their second, third, or fourth narrative feature. Participants work with leading experts on script development, legal and production strategy, career positioning, and receive psychological support throughout the creative process. Applicants must identify as a woman or gender-expansive filmmaker, work as a screenwriter, director, or producer, have completed at least one feature, and submit a project with a producer attached. Accommodation and meals are provided, but travel is not covered, and a participation fee of EUR 2,000 per person applies. At least one 50 percent scholarship is available annually for those with demonstrated financial need. The third edition runs October 25 to November 1, 2026, with applications due July 10, 2026.

grants.title: Loghaven Artist Residency
grants.location: Knoxville, Tennessee, USA
grants.amount: USD 850 weekly living stipend, travel subsidy USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 materials shipping reimbursement, plus room and board, housing in historic log cabins, and studio space USD 25 non-refundable application fee (fee waiver available on request)
grants.description: Loghaven Artist Residency offers practicing artists time, space, and resources on ninety wooded acres in Knoxville, Tennessee. Fellows stay in five rehabilitated historic log cabins with access to purpose-built studio space. Each Fellow receives an USD 850 weekly living stipend, a travel subsidy on a sliding scale from USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 in materials shipping reimbursement, and covered room and board. Residencies run from 2 to 6 weeks across sessions scheduled throughout 2027. The program is open to artists at any career stage working in architecture, dance, interdisciplinary art, music composition, theater, visual art, and writing, including collaborative groups of up to nine people. Applicants must be at least 21, live more than 120 miles from Knoxville, and be eligible to work legally in the US; international artists and degree-seeking students are not eligible.

grants.title: The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship
grants.location: New York City, USA
grants.amount: USD 5,000 grant plus writers studio space, mentorship, agent introductions, and community programming
grants.description: The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship annually supports nine early-career, New York City-based fiction writers at a pivotal point in their careers. Each fellow receives a 5,000 USD grant, dedicated space and time to write in the Writers Studio, personalized editorial mentorship, opportunities to meet literary agents, and community programming. Applicants must be current residents of one of the five boroughs and must remain in New York City for the entire fellowship year. An emerging writer is defined as someone who has not yet had a novel or short story collection published by a major or independent publisher and who is not currently under contract for a work of fiction; students enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship year are not eligible. The 2027 fellowship application window opens July 1, 2026 and closes July 31, 2026.

grants.title: Prisma Art Prize 20th Edition
grants.location: Rome, Italy
grants.amount: EUR 2,000 annual cash fund distributed across quarterly winners, plus group exhibition at Palazzo Merulana, inclusion in the collective catalogue, artist interviews, and eligibility for solo show prizes (Isorrophia Gallery, Pallavicini-Dettori) and partner art residencies (Dar Meso, Casacon, Osservatoriomaree) EUR 34 entry fee for 1 to 3 artworks, plus EUR 5 for each additional artwork up to a maximum of 8
grants.description: The Prisma Art Prize is an international award for painting and two-dimensional artistic research based in Rome. The 20th edition selects emerging and established artists working in painting, drawing, illustration, printmaking, and traditional mixed media for a group exhibition at Palazzo Merulana, opening November 5, 2026. Winners share an annual cash fund of EUR 2,000 distributed across quarterly calls and receive inclusion in the collective catalogue plus artist interviews. Additional opportunities include solo exhibition prizes and fully funded placements at partner art residencies. The prize is open internationally to all living artists regardless of age, gender, nationality, or educational background. The submission deadline for this edition is September 10, 2026.

grants.title: Dedalus Foundation Senior Fellowship 2027
grants.location: United States (remote, project-based)
grants.amount: Stipend up to USD 30,000
grants.description: The Dedalus Foundation, established by artist Robert Motherwell, awards a Senior Fellowship supporting critical and historical studies related to painting, sculpture, and allied arts of the twentieth century. The fellowship is open to art historians, critics, curators, and other writers and scholars who have demonstrated their abilities through previous accomplishments. Awards provide a stipend of up to USD 30,000 for projects lasting up to one year, with the amount varying by project needs. Applicants must be U.S. citizens and may not be currently pursuing academic degrees. Applications are submitted online and reference letters through a separate form. A distinguished scholar jury selects recipients, with awards announced by mid-December.

grants.title: Jentel Artist Residency
grants.location: Banner, Wyoming, USA
grants.amount: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee
grants.duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)
grants.description: The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

grants.title: 100 Voices of Florida Film Grant
grants.location: Florida, United States
grants.amount: Up to USD 5,000 (unrestricted) USD 25 application fee with a Florida connection, USD 35 without
grants.description: 100 Voices of Florida is a grant for filmmakers and screenwriters that awards up to USD 5,000 in unrestricted funds, which winners can use for finishing funds, festival submission fees, cast and crew reimbursements, production, or anything else. Applicants submit either a short film (under twenty minutes) or a script (twenty pages or less); films in other languages must include accurate English subtitles, and scripts must be in English. The grant is open to writers, directors, and producers who are at least 18 years old, and creative teams may submit, though only one entrant receives the award. A panel of judges with professional filmmaking experience evaluates entries, and the winner also receives a meeting with an industry professional. The deadline for the current cycle is September 20, 2026.

grants.title: Hayama Artist Residency
grants.location: Hayama, Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan
grants.amount: Fully funded: roundtrip airfare to Tokyo (Narita or Haneda), shared accommodation for 4 weeks, and USD 800 for meals and local transportation. Group exhibition at KOKI ARTS gallery in Tokyo. USD 95 application fee for first-time applicants
grants.description: The Hayama Artist Residency introduces visual artists from around the world to Japanese culture and offers them their first gallery exhibition in Japan. The four-week residency takes place in Hayama, a coastal town in Kanagawa Prefecture about 1.5 hours from Tokyo. Selected artists receive roundtrip airfare to Tokyo, shared accommodation with one other resident, and USD 800 for meals and local transportation. A core element is a group exhibition during the stay, hosted in partnership with KOKI ARTS, a contemporary gallery in the Bakurocho art district of Tokyo. The program is open to visual artists over 21 working in any medium, based anywhere in the world, and applications are reviewed by the director and an international selection committee. The June 2027 cycle has an application deadline of September 30, 2026, with notification by December 15, 2026.

grants.title: Open Interval: An Artist-Scientist Exploration of Turbulence
grants.location: United States (trio must be regionally co-located)
grants.amount: USD 30,000 per awarded trio
grants.duration: April to December 2027 (several months of collaboration)
grants.description: Open Interval is a Simons Foundation program that supports trios consisting of an artist, a scientist, and a representative from a 501(c)(3) arts organization to collaborate around a shared scientific theme. The current cycle centers on the theme of turbulence, a phenomenon at the edge of chaos and beauty. Awarded trios receive USD 30,000 to support a sustained period of thought partnership and exploration rather than the production of finished works or exhibitions. The program runs from April to December 2027 with a touchstone meeting in May 2027 and possible cohort convenings. Trio members must be regionally co-located anywhere in the United States so they can collaborate in person. The scientist should hold a Ph.D. and be conducting basic research, with priority given to fields such as astrophysics, neuroscience, quantum physics, biology, or mathematics. The application portal opens September 9, 2026, and all three trio members must submit for the application to be complete.

grants.title: The Studios at MASS MoCA Residencies
grants.organization: MASS MoCA (Massachusetts Museum of Contemporary Art)
grants.location: North Adams, Massachusetts, USA
grants.amount: Provides private 24/7 studio space, furnished housing, and seven communal meals per week. Financial aid is available so artists requesting it typically pay between USD 0 and USD 450 per week, and fully funded fellowships cover all residency fees for up to four weeks. Artists who fully self-fund pay a subsidized rate of USD 650 per week.
grants.description: The Studios at MASS MoCA is a year-round residency program on the museum's campus in North Adams, Massachusetts, hosting up to 10 artists at a time for stays of two or four weeks. It primarily welcomes visual artists across all media and writers in all literary disciplines, and applicants from around the world are encouraged to apply. Residents receive 24-hour access to a private furnished studio, lodging in nearby apartments, and several communal meals each week, plus museum membership benefits and access to The Clark Art Institute. The program offers need-based financial aid (artists typically pay between USD 0 and USD 450 per week) and fully funded fellowships that cover all residency fees for up to four weeks. General application deadlines fall on May 1 and November 1 each year, and the application is free to submit.

grants.title: Rauschenberg Medical Emergency Grants
grants.organization: New York Foundation for the Arts (NYFA) / Robert Rauschenberg Foundation
grants.location: United States (remote application)
grants.amount: One-time grants up to USD 5,000
grants.description: The Rauschenberg Medical Emergency Grants, administered by the New York Foundation for the Arts on behalf of the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation, provide one-time grants of up to USD 5,000 to help artists in financial need cover recent unexpected medical, dental, and mental health emergencies. The program is open to generative visual and media artists and choreographers working in visual arts, film, video, electronic and digital arts, and choreography who live anywhere in the United States, the District of Columbia, a Tribal Nation, or a U.S. Territory. Applicants must be artists in financial need, with adjusted gross income at or below USD 80,000 for an individual or USD 160,000 for joint filers, and must not have received a Rauschenberg Medical or Dancer Emergency Grant in the past five years. Eligible expenses include emergency medical bills, diagnostics, consultations, therapy, prescription medications, emergency dental care, and transportation to appointments. The grant does not cover lost wages, general living expenses, or care for anyone other than the artist. Applications are accepted during defined cycles, with the current cycle running roughly from late October to mid November.

grants.title: Farm, Flock and Fiber Artist in Residence
grants.location: Sumner, Maine, USA
grants.amount: Modest monthly stipend (around USD 400/month) in exchange for roughly 15 to 20 hours/week of farm work, plus free rustic private housing (solar power, wood stove), shared kitchen, bath and WiFi, community meals and farm-grown food, dedicated solo studio time, and weekly collaboration with resident fiber artist Marty Elkin. USD 25 non-refundable application fee
grants.duration: Six months beginning in May, with an optional two additional months for lambing and early lamb care
grants.description: A Wrinkle in Thyme Farm is a small, women-owned diversified farm in Sumner, Maine that offers a six-month live-in fiber arts residency starting in May. The resident exchanges an average of 15 to 20 hours per week of farm work for a modest monthly stipend, free private rustic housing, community meals, farm-grown food, and access to the fiber studio. Residents receive solo studio time plus one day a week of learning and collaboration with fiber artist Marty Elkin, working with home-grown Maine wool through felting, dyeing, knitting and spinning. The program also includes training in flock management, animal husbandry and lamb care, with the option to add two months for lambing season. It is designed for emerging fiber artists who are single females able to lift 50 pounds and work around a farm. Applications open in November with a December 31 deadline and a non-refundable USD 25 application fee.

grants.title: Helene Wurlitzer Foundation Residency
grants.organization: The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico
grants.location: Taos, New Mexico, USA
grants.amount: Rent-free, utility-paid furnished housing (casita); no stipend (rare stipend support via the Rhonda Artist Stipend Fund); artists cover food and travel USD 30 application fee
grants.duration: 10 to 12 weeks (Sessions 1 and 3 are 12 weeks, Session 2 is 10 weeks)
grants.description: The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico runs one of the oldest artist residency programs in the United States, established in 1954, on a 15-acre campus in Taos, New Mexico. The residency is open to visual artists, literary artists, and music composers, including painters, sculptors, photographers, filmmakers, writers, poets, playwrights, screenwriters, and songwriters. Selected grantees receive rent-free and utility-paid housing in a furnished casita for a session of 10 to 12 weeks, with access to a Commons House offering WiFi, a library, and bicycles. No stipend is provided in most cases, and residents cover their own food and transportation. Applications are open to creative artists worldwide regardless of age, experience, or nationality, and the annual deadline is January 18 for residencies the following calendar year. There is a non-waivable USD 30 application fee.

grants.title: Kala Fellowship Program
grants.location: Berkeley, California, USA
grants.amount: USD 3,000 stipend, 24/7 facility access for 1 to 9 months, 1 month of housing for artists outside the 9 Bay Area counties, a Kala class or workshop, and a group exhibition USD 25 application fee (free if applying December through January 15)
grants.duration: 1 to 9 months within the July 2026 to June 2027 fellowship period
grants.description: The Kala Fellowship Program supports international, national, and local artists producing innovative work in and across multiple media, with an emphasis on Kala's specialized printmaking, photography, and digital media equipment. The institute supports up to 6 artists per cycle with a USD 3,000 stipend, 24/7 access to its Berkeley facilities for 1 to 9 months, one month of housing for artists based outside the 9 Bay Area counties, a Kala class or workshop, and a culminating group show in the Kala Gallery. Applications are submitted through kala.slideroom.com and require a statement of interest, an artist CV, documentation of 5 to 10 recent works, and two references. Artists enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship period are not eligible. The application opens in December with no fee through January 15, after which a USD 25 application fee applies through the March 1 deadline.

grants.title: Roswell Artist-in-Residence Program
grants.location: Roswell, New Mexico, USA
grants.amount: USD 1,100 per month stipend, plus USD 250 per month materials stipend from the Frederick Hammersley Foundation. Includes a private house and a studio with rent, utilities, repairs, and maintenance covered. USD 26.32 application fee
grants.description: The Roswell Artist-in-Residence Program, established in 1967 and run by The RAiR Foundation, offers visual artists a year-long studio-based residency known as the Gift of Time. Each resident receives a private house and a studio on a fifty-acre complex, with rent, utilities, repairs, and maintenance covered by the program. Artists also receive a monthly stipend of USD 1,100 plus a USD 250 monthly materials stipend. The program is open to professional visual artists aged 21 and older working in painting, drawing, sculpture, printmaking, photography, video, digital media, and installation. It does not support performance art, writing, music, or production crafts. The application period for the next cycle runs from January 1 to March 15, 2027, and international applicants are accepted with visa support available.

grants.title: IDA Emergency Assistance
grants.location: Remote (worldwide applicants)
grants.amount: One-time emergency grant of USD 500 to USD 5,000
grants.description: The International Documentary Association runs an Emergency Assistance program that provides direct financial support to documentary filmmakers whose work puts them in imminent and extreme physical danger. Eligible filmmakers may request a one-time emergency grant between USD 500 and USD 5,000. IDA responds to all applications within 24 hours and handles them with strict confidentiality, with approved funds transferred in as few as five business days. Applicants need an existing risk assessment plan, a concrete safety plan, two references confirming the emergency, and a secure way to receive funds. The program launched as a pilot on March 9, 2026 and accepts applications on a rolling basis. For sensitive cases, applicants can contact emergency@documentary.org to share information over an end-to-end encrypted app such as Signal.

grants.title: ACA Artist Response Fund (ARF) Emergency Relief Grant
grants.location: United States (ACA member residencies)
grants.amount: Up to USD 1,500
grants.duration: One-time emergency grant; applications processed within about one week
grants.description: The Artist Response Fund (ARF) is an emergency relief program from the Artist Communities Alliance (ACA) for artists who experience threats to their safety or mental health during a residency and must leave before completing their term. Qualifying situations include assault, harassment, or systemic harm where the artist has attempted to address the issue without success. Grants of up to USD 1,500 help cover relocation costs such as transportation home, temporary lodging, supplies or basic needs, and rent upon returning. Eligible applicants are individual artists currently at, or having attended within the past 12 months, an ACA Organizational Member residency. There is no application fee, and the fund operates on a rolling basis with applications typically processed within one week. Artists apply directly through an online form and can also contact ACA staff with questions.

grants.title: Musicians Foundation Grants
grants.location: United States (remote application)
grants.amount: One-time emergency financial assistance; grant amount varies by need. Funds are issued as vendorized checks paid directly to billers (medical providers, landlords, utilities).
grants.description: The Musicians Foundation provides one-time financial assistance to professional musicians facing a specific time of need caused by an unexpected hardship such as a medical or dental situation, a natural disaster, or a housing crisis. Eligible applicants must be professional performers, composers/arrangers, or educators who have worked as professional musicians in the United States for five or more years, currently reside in the U.S., and have filed taxes as a musician for at least five years. The application has a rolling, always-open structure with a Pre-Application followed by a more detailed second part for those who qualify. Grants are issued as vendorized checks made payable directly to the biller and mailed to the applicant's home address. Processing typically takes between one and twelve weeks depending on volume.

grants.title: Adolph and Esther Gottlieb Foundation Emergency Grant
grants.location: New York, USA (applicants worldwide)
grants.amount: Up to USD 15,000 (USD 5,000 typical), one-time grant
grants.description: The Adolph and Esther Gottlieb Emergency Grant provides interim financial assistance to painters, sculptors, and printmakers facing an unforeseen, catastrophic event such as fire, flood, or a medical emergency. Applicants must have been engaged in a mature phase of their art for at least ten years and must have a current, urgent need that is beyond their means. The maximum award is USD 15,000, with USD 5,000 being typical, given as one-time assistance for a specific emergency. The program does not cover dental work, chronic situations, capital improvements, projects, general indebtedness, or lack of employment. There is no deadline; applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, usually within about four weeks, and within 24 to 48 hours in the most urgent cases.

grants.title: Adobe Creative Residency Community Fund
grants.location: Remote (online application)
grants.amount: USD 10,000 grant
grants.description: The Adobe Creative Residency Community Fund provides a one-time grant of USD 10,000 to creators experiencing times of extraordinary hardship. Recipients may use the funds at their own lawful discretion to support themselves and their families. Selection is made by Adobe's Corporate Social Responsibility team alongside subject matter experts in design, photography, and art, based on the applicant's professional portfolio and circumstances. There is no application fee and no project proposal is required. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis. Creators may apply to the Community Fund only once, and Creative Resident alumni are not eligible.

grants.title: CERF+ Emergency Relief Grants
grants.organization: CERF+ (The Artists Safety Net / Craft Emergency Relief Fund)
grants.location: United States and US Territories
grants.amount: USD 3,000 one-time grant
grants.description: CERF+ provides Emergency Relief Grants of USD 3,000 to craft artists in the United States who have experienced a recent and substantially disruptive career-threatening emergency or disaster. Eligible applicants are individual craft artists aged 18 or older who have lived and worked in the US or US Territories for the past two years and who make original three-dimensional visual art objects by hand, including folk and traditional art forms. The grant is a one-time non-taxable gift with no reporting required on how funds are used. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis, though the program will not fund applicants if too much time has passed since the qualifying event. There is no application fee. Artists may receive one grant per emergency, no more than one per calendar year, and up to four grants over their lifetime.

grants.title: Roman J. Witt Residency 2026-2027
grants.organization: University of Michigan Stamps School of Art and Design
grants.location: Ann Arbor, Michigan, USA
grants.amount: USD 20,000 honorarium plus housing, studio space, and up to USD 10,000 in materials, research and development funding USD 15 SlideRoom submission fee
grants.duration: Up to 12 weeks in residence served over an academic year
grants.description: The Roman J. Witt Residency is an annual program at the University of Michigan Stamps School of Art and Design, run in partnership with the University of Michigan Museum of Art and the U-M Institute for the Humanities. It awards one residency per academic year to a visiting artist or designer to produce a significant new work while engaging the university community and drawing on resources across campus. The program is open to both established and emerging artists and designers, and the ideal candidate values collaboration and creative partnerships across disciplines. Residents receive a USD 20,000 honorarium for up to twelve weeks in residence over an academic year, plus housing, studio space, and up to USD 10,000 in materials, research, and development funding. Applications are submitted through the SlideRoom online review system, which charges a nominal submission fee. The cycle typically opens in the fall with a January deadline; the 2026-2027 call closed on January 15, 2026, so prospective applicants should watch the official page for the next cycle.

grants.title: Basel House of Film Filmmakers Residency
grants.organization: Verein Filmhaus Basel und Umgebung (Basel House of Film)
grants.location: Basel, Switzerland
grants.amount: Housing provided plus a stipend to cover travel and living costs; no fee to participate
grants.description: The Basel House of Film, run by the Verein Filmhaus Basel und Umgebung, offers a three month residency for emerging artists working in film and television. Residents receive housing at the Filmhaus in the Basel old town plus a stipend to cover travel and living costs. There is no obligation to produce work, and residents can use the open schedule to write a script, prepare a film, draw storyboards, or edit. The facility includes a clubhouse, meeting room, office space, a small cinema with VR equipment, and an editing and grading suite. The program targets up and coming filmmakers, and both national and international applicants are welcome. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through the online form, followed by a CV, motivation letter, and portfolio sent to the residency email.

grants.title: ISCP Helen Frankenthaler Foundation Residency
grants.location: Brooklyn, New York, USA
grants.amount: USD 3,050 monthly stipend (roughly USD 18,300 over six months) plus a free private 24-hour studio
grants.description: The International Studio and Curatorial Program (ISCP) in Brooklyn runs a fully funded six-month residency supported by the Helen Frankenthaler Foundation for visual artists working in painting, drawing, and/or printmaking whose practice is marked by a spirit of innovation. Selected artists receive a USD 3,050 monthly living and materials stipend, 24-hour access to a private furnished studio, regular studio visits from visiting critics, participation in Open Studios, and field trips to museums and galleries. Applicants must be eligible to work in the United States and cannot be former ISCP residents. The program runs as an annual open call; the 2025 cycle closed on March 7, 2025 for a residency from September 2025 to February 2026, and a future cycle deadline had not yet been published as of mid-2026. There is no application fee. Applications are submitted by email to application@iscp-nyc.org with a CV, work samples, references, and supporting materials.

grants.title: Kaatsbaan Subsidized Residencies
grants.location: Tivoli, New York, USA
grants.amount: Generously subsidized residency; includes hotel-style on-campus rooms, locally sourced meals, and access to studios, a 160-seat black box theater, and a 153-acre Hudson Valley campus. No artist stipend.
grants.description: Kaatsbaan Cultural Park offers subsidized residencies for artists of all genres to create, live, and collaborate on its 153-acre campus in the Hudson Valley near Tivoli, New York. Residencies are individually designed between the artist, Kaatsbaan, and partners to fit the needs of the artist or company, welcoming disciplines from dance, theater, music, and visual arts to writing, composition, design, and film. The program is generously subsidized and includes individual hotel-style rooms, healthy locally sourced meals, and access to professional studios with sprung floors and pianos, a 160-seat black box theater, and exhibition space. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through an inquiry form, with early inquiries encouraged to secure preferred dates. Artists at any career stage worldwide may apply, individually or as collaborative teams. There is no application fee and no artist stipend.

grants.title: Grant Wood Fellowship
grants.organization: Grant Wood Art Colony, University of Iowa
grants.location: Iowa City, Iowa, USA
grants.amount: USD 40,000 salary plus benefits, furnished housing, and a private studio
grants.description: The Grant Wood Fellowship at the University of Iowa supports artists working in painting and drawing, printmaking, or interdisciplinary performance. Three fellowships are awarded each year through a competitive national selection process. Fellows hold a Grant Wood Visiting Assistant Professor appointment, receive a 40,000 dollar salary with benefits, furnished off-campus housing, and a private studio, and teach two courses during the academic year while pursuing their own creative research. Applicants generally need an MFA or equivalent, a national exhibition record, and university-level teaching experience. Applications open annually in January via jobs.uiowa.edu, with priority review beginning mid-January to early February and selections announced in April.

grants.title: The Factory on Willow Funded Artist in Residence
grants.location: Manchester, New Hampshire, USA
grants.amount: Furnished studio apartment with heat, hot water, electricity and internet, plus a USD 350 travel stipend and a USD 1600 stipend toward food and art supplies
grants.description: The Factory on Willow is a non-profit funded artist residency in Manchester, New Hampshire, housed in a 90,000 square foot mill building from 1904. The 12 week program supports amateur and emerging artists of any medium to develop their current projects and creative practice. Selected artists receive a furnished studio apartment with utilities and internet, a USD 350 travel stipend, and a USD 1600 stipend covering food and art supplies. The residency runs in three annual sessions (March to May, June to August, and September to November), with two artists selected per session. Each residency concludes with a public capstone showcase, and participants donate one work to the Factory's permanent collection. There is no application fee and applications are accepted on a rolling basis.

grants.title: Fire Island Artist Residency (FIAR)
grants.location: Cherry Grove, Fire Island, New York, USA
grants.amount: Free live/work space (lodging and studio), plus stipends for meals and travel and an honorarium USD 40 application fee
grants.description: Fire Island Artist Residency (FIAR) is the first residency in the United States exclusively for artists identifying as lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, non-binary, intersex, two-spirit, or queer. Each summer it provides free live/work space in the historic LGBTQ beach community of Cherry Grove on Fire Island, where emerging visual artists work, socialize, and receive studio visits, dinners, and discussions with renowned visiting artists and scholars over a four-week program. FIAR defines emerging as having completed fewer than two of the following: a solo show in a major art hub, a solo museum exhibition, or having work in a major institutional collection. The residency provides lodging, work space, stipends for meals and travel, and an honorarium. A USD 40 application fee applies. The 2026 cycle (July 10 to July 31, 2026, deadline April 1, 2026) has closed; the next annual open call is expected to be announced for the following summer season.

grants.title: Guggenheim Fellowship
grants.location: United States and Canada
grants.amount: Variable cash grant; awards are allocated based on the applicant's other resources and the scope of the proposed project, typically tens of thousands of US dollars per fellow.
grants.duration: Typically 6 to 12 months of supported work; flexible
grants.description: The Guggenheim Fellowship is an annual award from the John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation that supports exceptional individuals pursuing scholarship in any field of knowledge and creation in any art form. Fellowships are awarded on the basis of prior achievement and exceptional promise to mid-career professionals across more than 50 fields spanning the arts, humanities, natural sciences, and social sciences. Applicants must be citizens or permanent residents of the United States or Canada, and may apply individually or as a pair working jointly. Grant amounts vary and are adjusted to the fellow's other resources and the scope of their proposed work, with around 175 to 200 fellows selected each year. There is no application fee. The competition runs annually, with applications typically opening in late summer and closing in mid September for fellowships announced the following spring.

grants.title: The Cloud House Residency
grants.location: Minneapolis, Minnesota, USA
grants.amount: USD 300 per week stipend (prorated), plus free accommodation in a 700 sq ft one-bedroom cottage; possible studio space and travel/materials budget on request USD 30 application fee (plus USD 500 refundable deposit upon acceptance)
grants.description: The Cloud House Residency, run by Dreamsong in the Northeast Arts District of Minneapolis, provides accommodation and support for individuals working in contemporary art who intend to develop, exhibit, or curate new work in Minnesota. Residents stay in a 700 square foot one-bedroom cottage on Dreamsong's compound and receive a prorated stipend of USD 300 per week, with possible studio space and travel or materials budgets considered on request. The program is open to emerging and mid-career visual artists, curators, and writers with a focus on contemporary art, and it hosts one individual or a two-person collaborative team at a time. The organizers arrange introductions to local artists, curators, writers, institutions, and galleries across the Twin Cities. Standard stays are 4 to 6 weeks, with occasional one-week options. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, and the program is currently booking residents for October through December 2026 and throughout 2027.

grants.title: Steel Yard Micro-Residencies
grants.location: Providence, Rhode Island, USA
grants.amount: Free studio access (no residency fee). Optional Alloy Fellowship offers a USD 250 monthly stipend for the residency duration. Residents pay for their own materials and consumables. Housing not provided.
grants.description: The Steel Yard in Providence, Rhode Island offers micro-residencies for artists working in industrial art practice, specifically ceramics and metals such as blacksmithing, welding, and light metals. The program is designed to help an artist complete a specific project, commission, or prototype within a flexible timeframe of up to three months, offered year-round. Residencies are free of charge and include an all-access pass to the 10,000 square foot studio, tools, professional development, and exhibition opportunities, though residents cover their own materials. A limited number of Alloy Fellowships provide emerging artists a monthly stipend of USD 250 in exchange for five hours of monthly volunteer work. Applications run on a rolling basis, but the micro-residency application process is temporarily closed while the program undergoes restructuring, with updated guidelines to be announced.

grants.title: Pollock-Krasner Foundation Grant for Artists
grants.location: New York, USA (grants awarded internationally)
grants.amount: Up to USD 50,000 (unrestricted)
grants.description: The Pollock-Krasner Foundation awards unrestricted grants to professional visual artists who are painters, sculptors, and artists working on paper, including printmakers. Grants range up to USD 50,000 over a one-year period and can be used for both professional and personal expenses such as art supplies, studio rent, and living costs. Applicants must demonstrate recognizable artistic merit and financial need, and must be actively exhibiting their current work in professional venues such as galleries and museums. The Foundation does not fund students, commercial or graphic art, video, performance, film, or generative and AI-generated work. Applications are accepted on a rolling, year-round basis with no deadline, and the review process typically takes nine months to a year. There is no application fee, and all applications must be submitted online.

grants.title: Innovate Grant
grants.location: Online (applications accepted worldwide)
grants.amount: USD 1,800 unrestricted grant (two awarded per cycle: one to a visual artist, one to a photographer) USD 35 application fee
grants.duration: One-time unrestricted award; quarterly cycles (Winter, Spring, Summer, Fall)
grants.description: Innovate Grant awards two unrestricted USD 1,800 grants every quarter, one to a visual artist and one to a photographer, along with up to 18 honorable mentions who are featured and promoted on the organization's website. The award is unrestricted, so recipients can apply the funds toward new projects, materials, studio rent, travel, or other needs. Grant cycles run four times a year (Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). Visual artists and photographers aged 18 and older from anywhere in the world, both US and international, are eligible to apply. Applications require contact details, a grant category, and 3 to 10 images of work, plus a USD 35 application fee. The Spring 2026 cycle closed on June 18, 2026, and the Summer 2026 cycle follows on the regular quarterly schedule.

grants.title: Indigo Arts Alliance Mentorship Residency Program 2026
grants.location: Portland, Maine, USA
grants.amount: USD 2,000 stipend, plus furnished private apartment, round-trip travel to Portland, and 24-hour access to a 3,000 square foot community studio
grants.description: The Indigo Arts Alliance Mentorship Residency Program in Portland, Maine pairs artists of the African Diaspora and other Black and Brown communities for co-mentorship, collaboration, and creative exchange. Each pairing brings a National or International visiting artist together with a Local or Regional artist for a residency rooted in mentorship. The program welcomes all disciplines including painting, sculpture, writing, dance, music, theater, photography, and textiles. Indigo Arts Alliance provides a USD 2,000 stipend, a furnished private apartment, round-trip travel to Portland, and 24-hour access to a 3,000 square foot community studio. Applications are currently open on a rolling, year-round basis for artists residing in the North Eastern region (Maine, Vermont, New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island). Applicants must be 21 or older, have at least two years of studio or exhibition experience, and not be enrolled full-time in an academic program.

grants.title: Artist Residency Autumn 2026 (Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies, Palermo)
grants.organization: Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies
grants.location: Altarello di Baida district, Palermo, Sicily, Italy. On-site 62-day residency, 12 October - 12 December 2026. Open to all nationalities, but applicants must be officially residing in a Creative Europe country (excluding Italy).
grants.amount: Total allowance of EUR 1,860 (before tax) per artist, plus a travel budget of EUR 400 per artist (for distances under 4,000 km) with a EUR 400 green-mobility top-up, plus a production budget. Also provided: on-site accommodation in a fully furnished independent house (private bedroom; shared kitchen, living room, two bathrooms; outdoor studio space 100m from the working site), access to recycled/salvaged materials through the Centre's partnerships, a bicycle per resident, and a final public event.
grants.duration: 62-day residency, 12 October - 12 December 2026, structured in five phases (orientation, observation/fieldwork, proposal and ecological feasibility, creation, and public restitution).
grants.description: The Centre for Multispecies Relations and Future Ecologies invites applications from two artists for a 62-day residency (12 October - 12 December 2026) at its site in the Altarello di Baida district of Palermo, Sicily. The residency is structured around a 1,500 sqm garden at the threshold between the city's urban expansion and the historic rural landscape of the surrounding hills; the site, degraded through previous use (soil compaction and significant biodiversity loss), functions as both studio and subject, and artists participate in an ongoing process of ecological transformation, producing interventions that emerge from extended observation and fieldwork. Applications are welcome across all mediums and disciplines (installation, sound, performance, video, food art, participatory practice, and beyond); research-based and interdisciplinary practices are especially encouraged. The Centre works at the intersection of art, science, and (agro)ecological practices, with a focus on multispecies coexistence and climate change in the Mediterranean, and has been based in Palermo since 2021. PROGRAMME: five phases (orientation with small trips and gatherings; observation with independent fieldwork and consultations with artists, curators, scientists, and anthropologists; proposal and ecological feasibility developed with mentors; creation; and public restitution with an open event, a workshop, and a public discussion). Each artist develops an independent site-specific intervention; the two residents share the site, living space, and process, with exchange encouraged but not prescribed. Artist duos and artists who want to collaborate are encouraged to apply (you can apply as an individual for one position, as a duo for both, or as two artists collaborating for two positions). Mentorship by co-founders/artistic directors Fabio Aranzulla and Luca Cinquemani (weekly site visits, studio discussions, work-in-progress presentations); logistics managed by Francesca Farris. WHO: artists whose work engages with ecology, multispecies relations, or the more-than-human, who can move between observation and making and are interested in dialogue with scientists, land workers, and local communities. Priority is given to artists from working-class and less-privileged backgrounds, those who self-identify as part of marginalised communities, and candidates reflecting diversity in gender, age, geographical background, and ability; green travel is prioritised as a condition of participation where feasible. Applicants should be open to living and working in a neighbourhood on the margins of Palermo's periphery. Funded by Culture Moves Europe (Creative Europe programme of the European Union). APPLICATION: first phase via the online form (opened 10 June); shortlisted artists contacted before the end of July; second phase is video calls with shortlisted candidates; applications accepted in English and Italian. Contact: contact.cmrfe@gmail.com. Deadline 5 July 2026.

grants.title: Connections Through Culture Grants 2026 (British Council)
grants.location: International collaboration grant. Each application must involve at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they apply from throughout the project.
grants.amount: Grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000. Free to apply.
grants.duration: Project-based; develop and deliver collaborative projects across a range of themes and art forms.
grants.description: Connections Through Culture (CTC) grants are designed to enable meaningful partnerships, cross-cultural exchange and long-term collaboration between the UK and participating countries. In 2026, CTC incorporates the grants programmes formerly known as Biennials Connect (Visual Arts) and Architecture, Design and Fashion Biennials and Festivals Grants, and serves as the British Council's major Arts grants programme for 2026 (in place of International Collaboration Grants). It offers grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000 to support creative practitioners, cultural professionals, artists, organisations, festivals, biennials, hubs and collectives to develop and deliver collaborative projects across a wide range of themes and art forms, including applied arts, architecture, curating, dance, design, digital, drawing, education, fashion, film, installation, interdisciplinary work, painting, performance, photography, printmaking, public art, research, sculpture, social practice, sound art, textiles, video, visual arts and writing. ELIGIBILITY: applications must involve collaboration between at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they are applying from at the time of application and throughout the project activity period. PARTICIPATING COUNTRIES: Albania, Argentina, Australia, Bangladesh, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Colombia, Mainland China, Egypt, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Kenya, Kosovo, Lebanon, Malaysia, Mexico, Montenegro, New Zealand, Nigeria, North Macedonia, Pakistan, Palestine, Peru, Philippines, Saudi Arabia, Serbia, South Africa, Tanzania, Thailand, Tunisia, Turkey, United Arab Emirates, UK, Ukraine, Uzbekistan, Venezuela, and Vietnam. Eligible applicants include artists and creative practitioners, arts and cultural organisations, festivals and biennials, cultural professionals, creative hubs/networks/collectives, and organisations working across arts, culture and the creative sectors. Note: applications involving projects organised by or taking place as part of Visual Arts, Architecture, Design, Fashion and Craft festivals should be made by the festival or biennial organisation rather than by individual artists, designers or practitioners. No application fee. Deadline 12 August 2026.

grants.title: Upe Foundation Curatorial Fellowship at Camden Art Centre 2026
grants.organization: Upe Foundation (in partnership with Camden Art Centre)
grants.location: London, United Kingdom. Full-time, in-person; relocation to London is required. Applicants must hold the right to work in the UK.
grants.amount: Salary of GBP 30,960 per year. A full-time Assistant Curator role on a 12-month fixed-term contract, with the possibility of a further 6-month extension (18 months total) subject to formal review and mutual agreement.
grants.duration: 12-month fixed-term position starting Autumn 2026, with possible 6-month extension (18 months total).
grants.description: Upe Foundation is launching the Upe Foundation Curatorial Fellowship at Camden Art Centre as part of its Fall 2026 Curatorial Fellowship Programme. The opportunity invites Baltic early-career curators to apply for a full-time curatorial role at Camden Art Centre, an institution internationally acclaimed for its rigorous exhibitions, residencies, events and learning programmes. The fellowship is a full-time, 12-month position starting Autumn 2026, with the possibility of a further 6-month extension (18 months total) upon review and mutual decision; relocation to London is required. ROLE: embedded within Camden Art Centre's Exhibitions Department, the Fellow works as Assistant Curator, supporting the development, coordination and delivery of the exhibitions programme and related projects and events, with practical experience across exhibition research, production, interpretation, publications, public programmes, loans, installation and documentation, and liaising with artists, galleries, lenders, partner organisations, writers and designers. The Fellow also shares research connected to Baltic and broader Central Eastern European artistic contexts. ELIGIBILITY: a demonstrated connection to or sustained engagement with the Baltic region (Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, or their diasporas), which may include lived experience, heritage, working knowledge of a Baltic language, relevant research, or curatorial work with Baltic artists/institutions/contexts; early-career curatorial experience (freelance, self-organised, project-based or institutional); and a higher education qualification (BA, MA or equivalent) in curating, art history, visual culture or related disciplines. Desirable: working knowledge of Lithuanian, Latvian or Estonian; fluency in a second language; experience with Articheck and with CRM systems (particularly Salesforce). Applicants must hold the right to work in the UK (via the under-26 'new entrant' Skilled Worker route, the Creative Worker visa route, or existing right to work such as British citizenship or settled/pre-settled status). OFFER: GBP 30,960 salary, structured professional development within the Exhibitions team, and opportunities to build professional networks in London and internationally. Applications reviewed jointly by Upe Foundation and Camden Art Centre, with first-round online interviews and a second interview for finalists. The organizers strongly encourage applicants to write materials in their own words and avoid generic or AI-generated responses. TIMELINE: application deadline 12:00 noon UK time, Monday 13 July 2026; interviews 6 August 2026; final decision mid-August 2026; start Autumn 2026. Questions: recruitment@camdenartcentre.org.

grants.title: Open Call 2027: Galerie mladych (Galerie TIC Brno)
grants.location: Brno, Czech Republic. Exhibition projects realized at Galerie mladych during 2027.
grants.amount: The gallery covers the financial costs of exhibition installation, an artist/curator fee, collection and transport of the exhibition, and basic PR (photodocumentation included).
grants.description: Galerie TIC announces an open call for exhibition projects for Galerie mladych (Gallery of Young Artists) for the year 2027. Applicants should send a completed application form, a project description with visual documentation, and an artist's portfolio with CV by midnight on 20 July 2026 via the online form. THE GALLERY OFFERS: to cover the financial costs of exhibition installation; an artist/curator fee; collection and transport of the exhibition; and basic PR (photodocumentation included). APPLICATION: the application form, a short description and visual documentation of the artistic/curatorial project, and a portfolio with CV. Results will be published on the gallery's website and social media no later than the end of August 2026. Deadline 20 July 2026.

grants.title: TED & POSCA Global Artist Residency 2026: Visible Mending
grants.location: Globally distributed; no single location or central studio. Selected artists work within their own communities, with projects unfolding in public space.
grants.amount: Each selected artist receives a total of USD 15,000 (covering artist fees, production costs/community activation, and documentation), plus a production budget and tools from POSCA, creative direction and storytelling support, and visibility through TED and Fine Acts' global platforms.
grants.duration: One-year residency term; community-rooted public interventions developed in 2026 for implementation in 2027. The initiative runs for multiple years (2027, 2028, 2029), expanding the cohort year by year.
grants.description: TED (the global platform for ideas and storytelling) and POSCA (a creative brand by uni Brands Corporation) are launching a new kind of artist residency: a globally distributed cohort of artists working across different geographies, cultures, and communities, united by a shared drive to shape public life through art. Fine Acts, the external curatorial partner selected by TED & POSCA, leads the curation, creative direction, and coordination. There is no single location and no central studio: selected artists work within their own communities, following a shared creative constraint (a unifying conceptual framework around repair, hope, and beauty), with projects unfolding in public space, community participation as a key part of the work, and process mattering as much as outcome. 2026 THEME, VISIBLE MENDING: inspired in part by Kintsugi (the Japanese practice of repairing broken ceramics with gold), the theme explores repair as a creative and civic act, inviting artists to consider how wounds, ruptures, and imperfections can become sites of transformation, and to make processes of repair visible through public interventions that spark reflection, dialogue, and collective imagination. In 2026, four artists will be selected to develop community-rooted public interventions to be implemented in 2027. AWARD: each selected artist receives a total of USD 15,000 to cover artist fees, production costs/community activation, and documentation, plus a production budget and tools from POSCA, creative direction and storytelling support, and visibility through TED and Fine Acts' global platforms. While the residency term is one year, the initiative will run for multiple years (2027, 2028, 2029), expanding the cohort year by year and building an evolving global archive of civic artistic interventions. Apply by 31 July 2026.

grants.title: Wassaic Project 2027 Summer Exhibition Open Call
grants.location: Wassaic, New York, United States (in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills).
grants.amount: A modest honorarium for all accepted artists (part of the organization's effort toward W.A.G.E. certification). Artists creating site-specific installations are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship and a no-fee residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.
grants.duration: The 2027 Summer Exhibition runs free and open to the public every weekend, 1 May to 12 September 2027. Optional fellowship residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.
grants.description: The Wassaic Project is holding its annual Open Call for the 2027 Summer Exhibition, for artists of all mediums, including 2D work, sculpture, video, new media, site-specific installation, performance, text, poems, essays, and publication-specific work. Selected work is showcased alongside a diverse range of pieces and performances in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills; the 2027 Summer Exhibition will be free and open to the public every weekend from 1 May to 12 September 2027. In an effort toward W.A.G.E. certification, all accepted artists receive a modest honorarium to participate. Artists interested in creating a site-specific installation are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship to help realize their work; Fellows are offered a no-fee residency for 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027 (artists interested in making site-specific work should still apply regardless of whether they are interested in or able to be in residence). The Wassaic Project cultivates and supports community for emerging and professional contemporary artists, writers, and other creatives, with a residency program housed in historic landmark buildings that hosts 10 artists each month in the heart of a rural community. APPLICATION: requires application forms, a CV, and an artwork/portfolio; submitted via SlideRoom. Application fee: USD 25 (no participation fee or additional fees). Deadline 10 August 2026.

grants.title: Call to Artists: Walden Fire Station and Library (Calgary Arts Development)
grants.location: Walden community, Calgary, Alberta, Canada. Open to local and national (Canadian) professional artists/artist teams.
grants.amount: Total project budget of CAD 275,000 (exclusive of all applicable taxes) for one selected artist/artist team. The artist fee for the total project is CAD 68,750 + GST. Budget breakdown can be adapted to the artist's approach, following CARFAC's recommendation that a minimum 20% of the budget go to the artist's fee; the total allocated budget will not be increased.
grants.duration: Concept and design from September 2026 to end of July 2027; fabrication and installation (Phase 2) from the second half of 2027 into 2028.
grants.description: Calgary Arts Development (CADA) is seeking an artist or artist team to join The City of Calgary's Walden Fire Station and Library project team to create permanent public artwork(s) for a combined fire station and library planned for the community of Walden. One artist/artist team will be selected with a total project budget of CAD 275,000 (exclusive of all applicable taxes); the opportunity is open to local and national professional artists/artist teams working in various media. ELIGIBILITY: local and national mid-career to late-career professional public artists and artist teams with a minimum of five years' previous experience creating artwork in the public realm; they must have at least three outdoor permanent public art installations, each with a lifespan of over 10 years and a minimum project budget of CAD 150,000 (see the Canada Council for the Arts definition of a professional artist). Walden does not currently have an active community association, so the project requires experience in artist-led community engagement and a strong understanding of alternative outreach. Artists must be available to meet the project timelines from September 2026 to the end of concept and design by July 2027; fabrication and installation take place in Phase 2, starting in the second half of 2027 and continuing into 2028. REWARD: total project budget CAD 275,000 (artist fee CAD 68,750 + GST); CADA is open to adapting the budget breakdown to the artist's approach, following CARFAC's recommendation of a minimum 20% to the artist's fee, though the total budget will not be increased. TIMELINE: applications open 16 June 2026 (9am MT); pre-recorded information session posted early July; application deadline 28 July 2026 (no applications accepted after 1pm MT); shortlisted interviews mid-September; results late September 2026. Eligibility questions: walden@calgaryartsdevelopment.com.

grants.title: Windgate Artist-in-Residence Fall 2026 (Arkansas Tech University)
grants.organization: Arkansas Tech University (supported by the Windgate Charitable Foundation)
grants.location: Russellville, Arkansas, United States (Russellville campus, in the Arkansas River Valley, about one hour from Little Rock). In-person, full-time residency.
grants.amount: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes) plus USD 18,000 for supplies. Housing provided (a 2-bed, 2-bath campus apartment) and a large, climate-controlled, dedicated off-campus studio space.
grants.duration: Five-month residency over the Fall academic term 2026.
grants.description: The Artist-in-Residence (AIR) program at Arkansas Tech University is an immersive experience within an art and educational community, intended to advance the careers of practicing artists, present residents as role models for the ATU community, and strengthen public awareness of the significance of making art and its effect on the surrounding community. The residency takes place over a five-month period, giving the artist time and space to focus on the creative process. For Fall 2026, the program will select an artist to create a public artwork that enhances the ATU campus; both two-dimensional and three-dimensional proposals will be considered. The program is supported by a generous grant from the Windgate Charitable Foundation. The Department of Art at ATU offers programs in Fine Art, Graphic Design, Game and Interactive Media Design, and Art Education, with a facility in Norman Hall featuring a professional gallery, computer lab, lecture hall, and studios for painting, drawing, printmaking, sculpture, ceramics, and graphic design. RESIDENCY DETAILS & EXPECTATIONS: residents must be artists with significant exhibition records, strong communication skills, sustained studio practice, and a desire to engage with the larger campus community; involvement may include guest lecturing, public talks, mentoring students, open studios, and/or collaborating with faculty and students. The resident sometimes teaches one course in 2D Design, 3D Design, or Introduction to Drawing during their stay when enrollment allows. Artists are expected to commit as a full-time resident for the Fall academic term 2026. BENEFITS: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes), USD 18,000 for supplies, a 2-bed/2-bath campus apartment, and a large climate-controlled off-campus studio. Deadline 3 July 2026.

grants.title: MSB Interpretive Panels: Public Art Commission (Creative Philadelphia)
grants.organization: Creative Philadelphia (Office of Arts, Culture and the Creative Economy)
grants.location: Municipal Services Building, Center City, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States. Philadelphia-based artists strongly encouraged; artists from outside the region considered. Multiple in-person site visits required (travel costs covered within the project budget).
grants.amount: Total project budget of USD 250,000, which must cover: artist fees (18% design fee), design and engineering, fabrication and installation, structural footings/anchoring, travel and site visits, AR development (software, hosting, content production), insurance (1%), community engagement participation, and a service warranty keeping content compatible with new mobile OS versions for a minimum of 10 years. No application fees.
grants.duration: Permanent public artwork commission; two-part selection process.
grants.description: Creative Philadelphia announces a call to commission a series of permanent interpretive panels with integrated augmented reality (AR) components at the Municipal Services Building in Center City Philadelphia. The commission is part of a broader initiative that includes an upcoming public sculpture of Dr. Sadie T.M. Alexander by artist Vinnie Bagwell. Inspired by Alexander's groundbreaking legacy in public service, these interpretive panels will celebrate the City of Philadelphia's municipal employees (past, present, and future) and provide deeper context for understanding Dr. Alexander's impact; they will be located in front of the building's landscaped areas, flanking the upcoming sculpture, and are intended to complement and support the overall site experience. SCOPE: an artist team will design and fabricate four (4) permanent, sculptural interpretive panels incorporating augmented reality (AR) elements to expand storytelling beyond the physical surface of the artwork. ELIGIBILITY: all interested artists are invited to apply; Philadelphia-based artists are strongly encouraged, though artists from outside the region will be considered; multiple in-person site visits are required and associated travel costs must be covered within the project budget. SELECTION: a two-part process: Part 1 submission of qualifications; Part 2 finalist proposals and interviews. Up to three (3) finalists are invited to submit short concept proposals and attend an interview; at no point should artists submit design proposals. BUDGET: USD 250,000 total (see amount for the required cost breakdown). An Artist Informational Meeting is held via Zoom on 30 June, 6:00-7:00pm. Application questions: paige.phillips@phila.gov. Apply via Submittable. Deadline 12 July 2026 (submissions close Monday 13 July 2026, 6:00 AM).

grants.title: NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency 2026-2027
grants.organization: Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU), Art + Design Department
grants.location: Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center, Northeastern Illinois University, Chicago, Illinois, United States. In-person; semester-long access to NEIU facilities.
grants.amount: USD 800 honorarium, plus a semester of full access to NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to the project (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture); supplies can be requested with guidance and approval from Studio Area personnel. Visiting Artists may also exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery.
grants.duration: One semester. Residency Session 1: 25 January - 14 May; Session 2: 30 August - 11 December.
grants.description: The NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency strives to create visible connections between Northeastern Illinois University's diverse population of art and design students and working artists who embody these identities, providing insights and inspiration through a dynamic exchange of ideas and techniques. Visiting Artists should demonstrate a commitment to their artistic practice and an interest in engaging with NEIU's students and creative community. Each Visiting Artist is awarded an honorarium and has full access for a semester to the NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to their project; over the residency they showcase their work in a public academic forum, lead a hands-on workshop, and participate in a Portfolio Review Day for NEIU's Art + Design students. FACILITIES (in the Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center) include a Design Studio with the largest collection of Riso drums at any Chicago university, a Fab Lab (FDM and resin 3D printers, large-format CNC router, vinyl cutters, laser cutters), a Ceramic Studio (throwing wheels, glaze room, electric and gas kilns, raku area), a Letterpress Studio (two Vandercook presses, metal and wood type), a Sculpture Studio (wood shop, clay modeling and spray painting areas), a Photography Studio (analog and digital, plus a lighting studio), and fully equipped Painting, Drawing, and Printmaking studios. BENEFITS: USD 800 honorarium; facility access based on project focus (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture), with supplies requestable subject to approval; and the opportunity to exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery. PARTICIPATION: lead a 2-hour hands-on workshop for 15-20 students on a topic relevant to the project; give an hour-long artist talk with Q&A; and serve as a guest panelist for a day-long portfolio review. KEY DATES: open call 2 June - 9 August; emailed acceptances in October; Residency Session 1 runs 25 January - 14 May and Session 2 runs 30 August - 11 December. Apply via the JotForm application. Deadline 9 August 2026.

grants.title: Art Shanty Projects 2027: Call for Performances + Art Actions
grants.location: Bde Uman (Lake Harriet), Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States. On-ice program plus several mandatory in-person meetings in Minneapolis; in-person participation required.
grants.amount: Sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 (request an amount that fits the project: roughly USD 1,000 for full-day/most-ambitious activities, USD 500 for half-day, USD 250 for short or one-off/solo/low-production activities). Plus direct production support, discounted materials and resource sharing, a shared warming shanty, a hot meal, snacks, hot beverages, hand/toe warmers, one parking space per project, on-site medic, and ASL interpreter/audio describer for the public. No application fees.
grants.duration: On-ice program every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027, with production meetings and mandatory check-ins from September 2026 onward.
grants.description: Art Shanty Projects seeks winter enthusiasts and creative adventurers to create performances and art actions for its 2027 Program on Bde Uman (Lake Harriet) in Minneapolis. It invites music-makers, poets, builders, scientists, dancers, puppeteers, writers, dreamers, activists, outdoors-people, conversationalists, craftspeople, storytellers, and more to propose new and innovative ways to engage artistically with the public in the social context of a village on a frozen lake (or winter 'beach', as climate change may necessitate). ASP has an expansive notion of what constitutes a performance or art action, having welcomed dance, creative sporting events, happenings, carnival games, music, roving dance, temporary sculpture, puppet shows, artful haircuts, advice booths, climate actions, and more. NOTE: this call is for PERFORMANCES + ART ACTIONS only (a separate call exists for shanty artists). Since 2004, Art Shanty Projects has created joyful winter art experiences inspired by pop-up ice fishing villages, using the frozen lake as a public platform for a temporary creative community with tens of thousands of annual visitors. ASP is deeply committed to social, racial, gender, and economic justice and to accessibility (embracing the spirit of the ADA). ELIGIBILITY: everyone is eligible (artists at all career stages); applicants need not have experience with everything asked, but should show eagerness to engage, develop new skills, and stay open to new perspectives. Projects that involve selling anything or the exchange of legal currency will not be accepted. COMPENSATION: sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 plus extensive production and logistical support. PROGRAM: on-ice every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027; selected artists notified mid-August 2026, with mandatory in-person meetings (at least one team member) starting 10 September 2026. Apply via the Google form (preferred) or the no-Google-account alternative. Contact Erin Lavelle, Artistic Director: program@artshantyprojects.org. Deadline for proposal submissions: 9 July 2026, 11:59pm CST.

grants.title: Eamonn McCabe Bursary 2026 (Royal Photographic Society)
