grants.organization: The Royal Photographic Society (RPS), in partnership with Eamonn McCabe's family and The Guardian
grants.location: International bursary. Applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. Interviews held via Zoom.
grants.amount: GBP 3,000 to deliver a photography project on the theme of 'sporting endeavour'. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS, who will work with the recipient to bring the project to a wider audience.
grants.duration: The project should be completed within one year of being awarded.
grants.description: The Eamonn McCabe Bursary, launched in 2024, commemorates the life of photojournalist Eamonn McCabe (1948-2022), who made his name as a sports photographer before moving to wider editorial work, mainly for The Observer and The Guardian, and who supported a generation of photographers from the 1980s to the 2020s. Run by the RPS in partnership with McCabe's family and The Guardian, this international bursary continues that legacy. AWARD: GBP 3,000 to a photographer aged 30 years or under to deliver a photography project responding to the theme of 'sporting endeavour', which can be interpreted as widely as the applicant wishes, though the general approach should be suitable for publication. The project should normally be new, not previously exhibited or published. As well as professional sports, the call particularly encourages projects covering grassroots and community activities (for example, addressing issues such as mental health or supporting displaced communities), and welcomes creative interpretations of the theme. The completed project will be published by The Guardian and the RPS. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be aged 30 years or under on the closing date. PROCESS: interviews via Zoom in September; recipient announced by the end of September; project completed within one year of award. Apply via the RPS application portal using RPS Account credentials. Closing date 3 August 2026.

grants.title: Textile Exchange x Atmos: Fashion Regenerated 2026
grants.location: Open call; work to be promoted globally through Textile Exchange and Atmos and exhibited at the 2026 Textile Exchange conference.
grants.amount: Overall winner: a paid collaborative commission worth USD 10,000 with Textile Exchange to develop a new body of work documenting the evidence of regeneration, published through Atmos and Textile Exchange and shown at the 2026 conference. Two runners-up: work promoted by Textile Exchange and displayed at the conference exhibition, plus a USD 2,000 cash prize.
grants.description: Fashion Regenerated is an open call from Textile Exchange and Atmos. Winners are selected by a panel of expert judges and, for the first time, a public vote. The shortlist is promoted through Textile Exchange and Atmos, amplifying inspirational stories to an engaged audience of thousands. The overall winner receives a paid collaborative commission to the value of USD 10,000 with Textile Exchange to develop a new body of work documenting the evidence of regeneration, working closely with the team to identify the right story and access; the winning work is published through Atmos, Textile Exchange, and shown to an audience of more than 1,300 changemakers at the 2026 Textile Exchange conference. Two runners-up have their work promoted by Textile Exchange and displayed at the conference exhibition, and there is also a USD 2,000 cash prize. Check the call site for full eligibility and submission details. Deadline: 20 July 2026.

grants.title: Core Echo 2027: International Open Call & 3-Week Residency
grants.location: On-site residency, likely in China (organizer uses WeChat, stipend in RMB, and works enter a partner enterprise's collection; exact city not stated in the call). International open call.
grants.amount: Initial stipend of RMB 20,000 per selected artist. Round-trip travel, accommodation, and daily meals provided during the on-site residency. Dedicated co-creation studios provided (with supplementary temporary creation zones from partner enterprises). One piece from each artist's exhibited series enters the permanent collection of the partner enterprise, per a contract signed by the end of the residency.
grants.description: Core Echo 2027 is an international open call from Grandtek Art Center for a three-week on-site artist residency. SUPPORT PACKAGE: each selected artist receives an initial stipend of RMB 20,000, with round-trip travel, accommodation, and daily meals provided during the on-site residency. One piece from each artist's exhibited series will enter the permanent collection of the partner enterprise, with terms specified in a contract signed by the end of the on-site residency period. Dedicated co-creation studios are provided, and selected partner enterprises offer supplementary temporary creation zones; spatial requirements requested by artists will be met to the fullest extent possible. Weekly artist salons are held during the residency for peer exchange and exhibition planning. The call is announced via the organizer's WeChat channel; check the source for full details and the application form. Deadline: 15 July 2026, 15:59 UTC.

grants.title: Justice + Joy Summer Research Fellowship 2026
grants.location: United States; fully virtual program. Capstone showcase tentatively in person (mid-September 2026), with travel and lodging covered.
grants.amount: USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful program completion, plus skills-based training, guest speaker sessions, and peer community. All travel and lodging for the in-person capstone are covered.
grants.duration: Seven-week virtual program, 9 July to 20 August 2026, with a capstone showcase mid-September 2026. Weekly Wednesday sessions 5:00-6:30 PM ET plus approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week.
grants.description: The Justice + Joy Research Fellowship is a seven-week virtual summer program for girls, young women, and gender-expansive young people of color (ages 18-26) in the United States who want to explore issues impacting their lives, communities, and futures. Fellows identify a topic they care about, such as mental health, education, reproductive justice, immigration, AI and technology, gender-based violence, beauty standards, young parenthood, or another issue affecting their communities, and develop an original research project grounded in their experiences, questions, and ideas. Throughout the summer, fellows participate in weekly virtual sessions, engage with guest speakers, build community with peers from across the country, and receive mentorship and support as they develop their research. The fellowship culminates in a capstone showcase where fellows present their work to the Justice + Joy community, including organizational leaders, partners, and supporters. No prior research experience is required; the program seeks young people who are curious, committed to learning, and interested in using research as a tool for understanding, advocacy, and change. COMMITMENT: weekly virtual sessions on Wednesdays 5:00-6:30 PM ET (9 July - 20 August 2026); approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week; required orientation on Wednesday 9 July; development of an individual research proposal or conceptual framework; presentation at the capstone showcase (tentatively in person, mid-September 2026, with all travel and lodging covered). WHAT YOU GET: a USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful completion; skills-based training in research methods, social justice frameworks, and community advocacy; guest speaker sessions; a peer community; and a real audience for the research (the Justice + Joy board and funders). Apply via the Google Form. Note: an explicit application deadline is not stated; the required orientation and program start is 9 July 2026, so apply before then.

grants.title: Artivism & Amplifiers Fellowship 2026
grants.location: Global; pairs young climate leaders from the Global North and Global South. Open to speakers of English, Spanish, French, and Portuguese.
grants.amount: USD 1,000 stipend upon completion. Supported by expert workshops and creative mentors.
grants.description: The Artivism & Amplifiers (A&A) Fellowship pairs young climate leaders from the Global North and Global South to co-create multimedia campaigns, exhibits, and installations for the Creative Climate Awards 2027. Over six months, fellows build skills in intercultural collaboration, climate storytelling, and community engagement, supported by expert workshops and creative mentors. The program is open to speakers of English, Spanish, French, and Portuguese. Fellows receive a USD 1,000 stipend upon completion. The organizers strongly encourage applications from people most impacted by the climate crisis, including those from the global majority, Indigenous, LGBTQIA+, immigrant, and diaspora communities, and people with disabilities. Applications close Wednesday 1 July 2026 at 9am GMT+2.

grants.title: Genera Short Film Fund 2026 (current round)
grants.location: Global; open to short filmmakers worldwide (talent drawn from over 176 countries).
grants.amount: Up to GBP 5,000 in funding for a short film project, any genre. Application requires either Pro Membership or a one-off funding fee (GBP 40 to GBP 50 depending on deadline tier).
grants.duration: Apply at any project stage: development, production, post-production, or festival circuit.
grants.description: Genera Films offers up to GBP 5,000 in funding for a short film project, regardless of genre. Applications are welcome at any stage of a project's evolution: initial development, production, post-production, or preparing for the festival circuit. Applicants can either be Pro Members or pay a one-off funding fee that ranges from GBP 40 to GBP 50 (Earlybird GBP 40, Regular GBP 45, Final GBP 50); Pro Membership offers additional benefits described on the Membership tab. Established in 2017, Genera Films champions short filmmakers worldwide, drawing talent from over 176 countries and having supported over 50 projects as an independent patron. CURRENT ROUND TIMELINE: round opens 2 January; Earlybird deadline 28 February (GBP 40); Regular deadline 30 April (GBP 45); Final deadline 30 June (GBP 50); shortlist announced 10 July; funded films announced 17 July.

grants.title: Sonic Connections Open Call 2027 (Villa Filanda Antonini)
grants.location: Villorba, Italy (a former silk mill). Addressed to artists and professionals NOT currently residing in Italy.
grants.amount: Fully funded residency covering mentoring, accommodation, travel costs, production costs, and a daily salary, plus a EUR 1,000 residency stipend.
grants.description: Villa Filanda Antonini (VFA) launches its Spring Open Call to select up to 4 artists and professionals for a one-month residency in March 2027 on the theme 'Sonic Connections', focused on experimental music, sound art, and performances. Hosted at Villa Filanda Antonini, a former silk mill in Villorba, Italy, the project brings together four international artists and professionals to examine the relationship between architecture, landscape, and collective sonic experience. ADDRESSED TO: artists, performers, curators, designers, and professionals not currently residing in Italy. GRANT & BENEFITS: a fully funded residency covering mentoring, accommodation, travel costs, production costs, and a daily salary, plus a EUR 1,000 residency stipend. WHEN: 1-31 March 2027. HOW TO APPLY: complete the application form and attach a CV, portfolio, project proposal, and letter of reference. The VFA residency program has been selected under conditions by Culture Moves Europe, a project funded by the European Union and implemented by the Goethe-Institut. Deadline: 19 July 2026, 23:59 CEST.

grants.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
grants.organization: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)
grants.location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).
grants.amount: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.
grants.description: The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

grants.title: Digital Democracy Centre x TrygFonden Fellowship Program 2027
grants.organization: Digital Democracy Centre (University of Southern Denmark), with support from TrygFonden
grants.location: Odense, Denmark (University of Southern Denmark). Flexible mix of in-office stays at the DDC in Odense and remote periods; stays planned to the Danish working calendar.
grants.amount: Either a salary subsidy or a stipend to help with travel and/or housing; specific benefits (financial support, access to DDC research resources, flexible working spaces, speaker series, networks) vary by agreement between the fellow, DDC, and partnering organizations. Applicants describe the posts they seek support for (flights, housing, research costs, salary compensation, stipend, etc.).
grants.duration: Flexible; typically 2-6 months (minimum two months, can include shorter DDC stays and remote periods), longer in exceptional cases. Fellowships start anywhere in 2027.
grants.description: The Digital Democracy Centre (DDC) at the University of Southern Denmark, with support from TrygFonden, is calling for applications for its 2027 fellowship cohort. The program fosters collaboration between researchers, journalists, and policymakers exploring the impact of digital technology and AI on media, politics, and democracy. Fellows collaborate with a self-selected DDC mentor to generate new knowledge, contribute to public debate, develop networks, and produce tangible outreach outputs such as policy briefs, educational packages, and talks. THREE FELLOWSHIP TYPES: (1) academic fellows researching digital technologies and democracy; (2) journalist fellows covering digital technology and democracy; (3) policy fellows contributing to the regulatory debate. Target group: researchers, journalism graduates, and policymakers in research/educational institutions, media organizations, ministries, NGOs, etc. PRIORITIES: projects with clear relevance to Denmark or Danish society (including comparative/international projects yielding insights relevant to a Danish context); in 2027, projects related to DDC's Hybrid Warfare Initiative (societal resilience to new forms of international conflict, with a focus on digital technologies and AI) are also prioritized. EXPECTATIONS: applicants must secure confirmation of mentorship from their desired DDC mentor BEFORE applying (applications without a confirmed mentor are automatically rejected; PhD students and research assistants are generally not eligible as mentors, and Sten Schaumburg-Muller is unavailable as a mentor for the 2027 call); applicants must also coordinate support from their current supervisors/managers. Fellows engage in DDC activities, seminars/workshops, and public outreach, and submit a short report within two months of finishing. APPLICATION (in English): preliminary project title, fellowship type, brief project description (max 1000 chars), detailed description and timeline (max 2400 chars, including duration, confirmed mentor, and remote/in-office work plan), description of activities and collaboration with external practice partners (max 1000 chars), expected product/impact/output (max 500 chars), a CV (max 5 pages), and an overview of the posts for which financial support is sought. Submit the application form (PDF or Word) plus CV as PDFs to ddc@sdu.dk with subject line 'DDC / TrygFonden Fellowship application'. Deadline 1 September 2026; decisions communicated no later than 1 October 2026.

grants.title: Creative Steps for Individuals 2026 (Arts Council of Wales)
grants.location: Wales, United Kingdom. For artists and creatives based in Wales.
grants.amount: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs). Applicants can apply for up to 90% of the developmental cost; the remaining 10% must come from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both).
grants.duration: Supports a stage of professional development; allow a minimum of 10 working weeks between the deadline and the project start date.
grants.description: Creative Steps funds the professional, business and organisational development of artists who have experienced barriers to careers within the arts. Creative Steps for Individuals is open to individual artists and creatives who identify as being Ethnically and Culturally diverse, D/deaf, disabled or neurodivergent, groups that are underrepresented in Arts Council of Wales funding programmes due to barriers to entry. Applicants must have demonstrable experience as an artist or creative professional; those without may still be supported on the basis of artistic potential by submitting a portfolio, CV or examples of work, discussed with a Relationship Manager. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: career development, NOT creating new work or project delivery. Examples include peer-to-peer learning, training courses (not formal/vocational education), building networks, mentorship/coaching, business development/social media/marketing advice, website development/marketing materials/equipment or materials, and setting up a new organisation. Budgets may include the applicant's own time, freelancer costs (paid at standard industry rates), mentor/consultant costs, access costs, and childcare/caregiving costs. AMOUNT: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs); up to 90% of the developmental cost, with the remaining 10% from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both, confirmed by letters of support). No application fee. APPLY: applicants must express interest at least 3 weeks before the deadline to receive support from an Application Coach or Access Support coordinator; an application form is provided by a Relationship Manager. The fund runs in rounds; the next round (Round 2 2026) has an Expression of Interest cut-off of 8 July 2026 and an application deadline of 29 July 2026. Later rounds: Round 3 (EOI 30 September, deadline 21 October 2026) and Round 4 (EOI 10 February, deadline 3 March 2027).

grants.title: SIAP: Major Individual Artists Award 2026/27 (Arts Council NI)
grants.location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.
grants.amount: Up to GBP 15,000 per award; four awards available in 2026/27. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).
grants.duration: Supports specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development and materials/equipment. Activity duration not specified.
grants.description: The Major Individual Artists award, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds extended or ambitious work by established artists, covering specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development, and materials/equipment. AMOUNT: four awards of up to GBP 15,000 each in 2026/27. ELIGIBILITY: established artists across all disciplines with national/international recognition who have not previously received a major award; academics and postgraduate artists are eligible if the project costs do not relate to their institutional work or studies. Applicants must contact the relevant artform officer before submission (by 10 July 2026); applications without this prior contact are ineligible. No application fee. The online application system closes precisely at the deadline and applicants must receive a confirmation email to verify successful submission. Deadline: Monday 20 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

grants.title: SIAP: Project Funding for Individual Artists 2026 (Arts Council NI)
grants.location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.
grants.amount: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).
grants.description: Project Funding for Individuals, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds project-based creative activities across all artistic disciplines. Eligible activities include creating new work, research and development, residencies, mentoring, professional development, and time dedicated to specific creative outcomes. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, artist fees/time, residency costs, travel (mileage at GBP 0.257/mile), venue hire, technical equipment (max 50% of request value), and IT equipment (laptops/PCs up to GBP 1,200; tablets up to GBP 500). ELIGIBILITY: artists of all disciplines and career stages, including emerging artists (less than 5 years professional experience), and in some cases technical staff/administrators; students and academics at third-level institutions may apply with departmental approval showing the costs are not covered by their employer or academic work. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon (online submission required).

grants.title: SIAP: Artists' International Development Fund 2026 (Arts Council NI)
grants.location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland; international development takes place abroad with overseas partners/hosts.
grants.amount: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).
grants.duration: Early-stage international development opportunities; residencies supported for a maximum of 4 weeks.
grants.description: The Artists' International Development Fund (AIDF), part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds early-stage international development opportunities for individual, freelance and self-employed artists to build networks abroad. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, travel, residencies (up to 4 weeks), technical fees, venue hire, and equipment, with IT devices capped at GBP 1,200 for laptops/PCs and GBP 500 for tablets. ELIGIBILITY: artists across all disciplines, particularly emerging artists (under 5 years professional experience); academics and students at third-level institutions may apply if the project differs from their employment/studies. AIDF applicants must have received recognition for their work in Northern Ireland, lack extensive international experience, and demonstrate interest in their work from an overseas partner/host. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

grants.title: Creative Scotland Open Fund for Individuals
grants.location: Scotland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be living in Scotland and hold a UK bank account in their own name.
grants.amount: Between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. Overall annual budget for the fund is GBP 5 million.
grants.duration: Supports research, development and/or delivery of creative activity (specific projects and productions) for up to 24 months.
grants.description: The Open Fund for Individuals is one of Creative Scotland's key funding programmes, supporting the wide range of activity initiated by artists, writers, producers and other creative practitioners in Scotland. The overall annual budget for this fund is GBP 5 million. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: periods of research, development and/or delivery of creative activity, such as specific projects and productions, for up to 24 months; it can support an individual's time where this is related to specific creative outcomes. Applicants are asked to give a start and end date for the activity and to describe the outcomes, benefits and impacts they wish to achieve. ELIGIBILITY: freelance and self-employed artists and creative practitioners living in Scotland who are at least 18 years old; applicants must have a UK bank account in their own name. Those who are not self-employed individuals or sole traders may instead be eligible for the National Lottery Open Fund for Organisations. FUNDING AMOUNTS: between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. TIMESCALES: requests between GBP 500 and GBP 20,000 receive an outcome in about 10 weeks; requests between GBP 20,001 and GBP 50,000 in about 14 weeks. This is a rolling open fund with no fixed deadline. Download the guidance for applicants and apply via the online portal.

grants.title: Exploratorium Artist-in-Residence Program 2026-2027
grants.location: San Francisco, California, United States. Residency is embedded within the Exploratorium; includes access to its facilities and staff.
grants.amount: USD 15,000 annual stipend, plus support for travel, project management, and financial support for residency projects, and access to Exploratorium facilities and staff expertise.
grants.duration: Residencies typically unfold over two years, including an exploratory phase and a project-development phase (room for variance).
grants.description: Since its inception in 1974, the Exploratorium's Artist-in-Residence Program (AIR) has grown to include hundreds of artists and performers. The museum works with individuals and artist groups who are drawn to collaboration, interested in interdisciplinary dialogue, and open to developing new working methods. Projects have taken countless forms, such as multimedia performances, theatrical productions, animated filmmaking, immersive installations, walking tours, and online projects. The program allows artists to embed within the unique culture of the institution, affords access to a dynamic and distinct staff, and provides opportunities for cross-pollination with a diverse public. While the museum allows room for variance, residencies typically unfold over two years and include both an exploratory and a project-development phase. The AIR program is an opportunity for artists to begin their residency with a sense of curiosity and experimentation rather than a fully formed idea, and to work in a co-creative manner with the Exploratorium. Applicants should be inherently curious and deeply invested in inquiry as part of their practice. AIRs receive a USD 15,000 annual stipend, support for travel, project management and financial support for residency projects, and access to Exploratorium facilities and staff expertise. APPLICATION TIMELINE: the form opened 20 May 2026 with a no-cost early-bird period; a USD 35 regular application fee applies from 10 June 2026; last day to submit is 29 June 2026; finalists notified and interviews scheduled September 2026; notifications sent to all applicants November 2026. Apply for the 2026-2027 program at the Exploratorium AIR page.

grants.title: Foundwork Artist Prize 2026
grants.location: Open call. No location restriction stated; submitted online via Foundwork.
grants.amount: The honoree receives an unrestricted USD 10,000 grant, studio visits with each of the five jurors, and an interview for Foundwork's Dialogues program. Three artists named to the Short List each receive a USD 1,000 micro-grant.
grants.description: The Foundwork Artist Prize is an annual juried award supporting outstanding artistic practices. The 2026 honoree receives an unrestricted USD 10,000 grant, studio visits with each of the five jurors, and an interview for Foundwork's Dialogues program. In addition, three artists are named to the 2026 Short List, each receiving a USD 1,000 micro-grant in support of their practice. Submissions are made online through Foundwork. Application deadline 25 September 2026.

grants.title: OpenAI Economic Research Exchange 2026
grants.location: Remote / global. Structured, project-based research collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research. No location restriction stated.
grants.amount: USD 25,000 project grant plus a USD 7,500 monthly stipend per selected researcher.
grants.duration: Carefully scoped projects with defined milestones. OpenAI supports a portfolio across time horizons: short-term projects (2-6 months) using existing approved data, and medium-term studies (6-12 months) that track outcomes over time or test specific interventions.
grants.description: The OpenAI Economic Research Exchange is a new program supporting high-impact external research on the economic effects of AI. Through the Exchange, selected researchers conduct work via structured, project-based collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research, with the goal of producing credible, independent evidence on how AI is affecting workers, firms, institutions, and the broader economy. OpenAI is seeking projects that address important questions about the economic impacts of AI; proposals should explain how carefully governed, privacy-protected use of OpenAI tools and datasets can help answer these questions, extending beyond what traditional datasets alone allow, while maintaining clear safeguards for user privacy and responsible data use. Core questions include labor market effects (moving beyond AI exposure to measure which occupations are likely to see augmentation, displacement, new task creation, or employment growth as model capabilities improve) and measuring economic value (willingness to pay, revealed value, task-level time savings, avoided costs, quality-adjusted output, and durable productivity gains). Selected researchers undertake carefully scoped projects with defined milestones, data governance, and review processes. WHO SHOULD APPLY: researchers with strong empirical skills and relevant expertise in applied causal inference, measurement, labor economics, productivity, firms, education, entrepreneurship, public finance, regional economics, development, inequality, or related fields. EVALUATION: proposals are assessed on methodological rigor, feasibility, fit with Exchange priorities, clear milestones, and the potential to contribute credible external evidence on AI's economic impacts. The Exchange builds on OpenAI's broader measurement efforts, including OpenAI Signals. For more information see the Request for Proposals, or contact econresearch@openai.com. Applications close 5 July 2026; selected researchers will be notified by 31 July 2026.

grants.title: AI Research Fellow / Senior AI Policy Researcher (Utah Office of AI Policy)
grants.organization: Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy
grants.location: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States. Hybrid telework may be available but regular in-person work is required, and in-state work rules require all work to be performed while physically within Utah. No visa sponsorship.
grants.amount: Salary: USD 33.13 to 54.01 hourly wage. Full-time, time-limited position. Job classification Research Consultant III (#16048); may underfill as Research Consultant II (#16058) with a reduced salary range.
grants.description: The Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy, is seeking an AI Research Fellow to help drive technical, scientific, and policy research for Utah's AI Learning Lab. This is an applied research and policy-translation role whose work should directly inform regulatory decisions, legislative recommendations, regulatory mitigation agreements, and stakeholder strategy. The role is technical and relies heavily on statistical, machine learning, computer science, and empirical research skills, while also requiring strong judgment, clear writing, and the ability to operate in a small multidisciplinary team alongside colleagues with policy, business, regulatory, and legal expertise. The Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy was created in May 2024 to systematically develop sensible AI policy across major impact areas such as healthcare, education, finance, transportation, and consumer technology. Central to the Office is the AI Learning Lab, where industry experts, academics, regulators, and policymakers work toward appropriate regulatory responses to emerging AI applications; the Office also has authority to create regulatory mitigation agreements that let responsible AI businesses test products in Utah under structured conditions while the state gathers evidence. KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: independently scope and execute research projects on emerging AI policy questions (defining the question, identifying technical and empirical evidence, interviewing experts, evaluating tradeoffs, and producing written recommendations); research the technical aspects, capabilities, limitations, and societal impacts of AI technologies; evaluate evidence and forecasts on the impact of human-AI interactions on health, economic activity, education, consumer welfare, and public institutions; draft executive policy memos, legislative briefings, technical explainers, risk assessments, and regulatory mitigation analyses; interface with business leaders, technologists, researchers, regulators, legislators, and lawyers; and advise on the development of regulatory and legal standards. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: deep technical knowledge of statistical methods, machine learning, and AI systems; strong ability to translate technical and empirical evidence into practical policy options for nontechnical decision-makers; familiarity with human-subject research, qualitative and quantitative methods, medical or health sciences, social sciences, human-computer interaction, or related empirical fields; excellent problem-solving, reliability, and independent judgment; and strong technical communication across policy, legal, regulatory, business, and technical audiences. PREFERRED: experience implementing, evaluating, or deploying AI systems (business, healthcare, government, or other applied settings); expertise in public policy, law, regulatory design, or government decision-making; ability to evaluate technical claims, research methods, and evidence quality; specific healthcare experience; and experience supporting AI deployment in regulated environments. A criminal history check is required. Recruiter: Tehra Gorski, tgorski@utah.gov, 801-530-6030. Application deadline 24 June 2026.

grants.title: AI Impact Scaling Program 2026 (Tech To The Rescue)
grants.location: NGOs and social impact organizations from around the world. Requires a project lead able to commit at least 5 hours per week over the 12-month program.
grants.amount: No-cost program. Participating organizations are estimated to receive between USD 150,000 and USD 330,000 worth of services at no cost, including a matched pro bono technology partner, scaling strategy support, a tailored AI roadmap, responsible-AI/cybersecurity/data-governance guidance, mentors, infrastructure credits, technical resources, and visibility with funders and investors via a global Demo Day.
grants.description: The AI Impact Scaling Program from Tech To The Rescue helps nonprofits and social impact organizations use AI to expand the reach and effectiveness of interventions that already demonstrate measurable results. The focus is scaling proven impact, not proving it. Selected organizations get access to an ecosystem designed to turn proven programs into scalable AI-powered solutions, including: a matched pro bono technology partner from a network of 2,000+ software companies and AI experts; scaling strategy support and a tailored AI roadmap; specialized guidance on responsible AI, cybersecurity, and data governance; access to mentors, infrastructure credits, and technical resources; and visibility with funders and investors through a global Demo Day. The program estimates participating organizations receive between USD 150,000 and USD 330,000 worth of services at no cost. WHO SHOULD APPLY: organizations with a proven intervention, real users, measurable outcomes, and clear potential to scale; NGOs and social impact organizations from around the world; teams able to dedicate a project lead for at least five hours per week over the 12-month program. You do not need to have an AI solution already built; the program helps identify where AI can accelerate impact and then build the solution alongside experienced technology partners. Application deadline 30 June 2026.

grants.title: Manuel António da Mota Prize – 17th Edition (2026)
grants.location: Portugal. Open to private social solidarity institutions and other non-profit entities working in eligible areas.
grants.amount: Total prize fund. The winning application receives a cash prize of EUR 50,000. Second and third place receive EUR 25,000 and EUR 10,000 respectively. The remaining 7 applications (honourable mentions) each receive a cash prize of EUR 5,000.
grants.description: In its 17th edition in 2026, under the motto 'Always in Solidarity,' the Manuel António da Mota Prize recognizes institutions that excel in the fight against poverty and social exclusion; the reception and integration of migrants and refugees; the development of rural areas and territorial cohesion; health; education; employment; family support; innovation and social entrepreneurship; inclusion and digital and technological transition; and climate transition. AWARDS: the winning application receives a cash prize of EUR 50,000; second and third place receive EUR 25,000 and EUR 10,000 respectively; the remaining seven applications (honourable mentions) each receive EUR 5,000. The prize is run by the Fundação Manuel António da Mota. Applications are open until 30 June 2026. See the foundation's current-edition page for full rules and eligibility.

grants.title: Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship 2026
grants.location: Open to students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities.
grants.amount: Up to USD 8,000 toward education.
grants.description: The Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship supports students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities. The award is worth up to USD 8,000 toward education. Apply via the Google Form linked below. Deadline 30 June 2026.

grants.title: Extra Teeth: Open Submissions for Fiction and Nonfiction (paid)
grants.location: Scottish magazine with an international outlook. Open to writers anywhere; Scottish writers especially championed, and submissions from underrepresented groups especially welcome.
grants.amount: GBP 200 per piece selected for the magazine, plus two copies of the issue. Four pieces are also selected for the With Bite Substack at GBP 150 each. Guest illustrator (one per issue) is paid GBP 943.90. Paid by bank transfer within 30 days of a signed publishing agreement. No submission fee.
grants.duration: Per-issue publication; submissions accepted during open submission periods.
grants.description: Extra Teeth is a Scottish literary magazine with an international outlook, paying for fiction and nonfiction. Submissions are currently open. No theme is set per issue; send your best work on any topic. Pieces should be 800 to 4,000 words, previously unpublished, submitted as a Word document (standard font, size 12, double line spacing) titled with 'F' for fiction or 'NF' for nonfiction followed by your name, plus a title sheet (name, address, word count, bio under 100 words); cover letters are not required. Send one piece only per submission period to submissions@extrateeth.co.uk with 'SHORT STORY: TITLE' or 'ESSAY: TITLE' in the subject line; secondary submissions are deleted. Copyright remains with the author. Work in translation is accepted with the necessary permissions. The magazine does not currently publish poetry, scripts or artwork (aside from its guest illustrator). PAYMENT: GBP 200 per piece for the magazine plus two contributor copies; four pieces are also chosen for the With Bite Substack at GBP 150 each. AI POLICY: no AI-written work is published or considered. ILLUSTRATORS: one guest illustrator is commissioned per issue (paid GBP 943.90); send work to hello@extrateeth.co.uk with 'ILLUSTRATOR' in the subject line; marginalised illustrators especially encouraged. Allow eight weeks after the submission period before querying. Check the submissions page for current open periods.

grants.title: AY-ROOP / Le Milieu: Reuse - (Re)making for Circus Residency 2026 (Rennes Métropole, France)
grants.location: Le Milieu, Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole, France. Applicants must legally reside in a Creative Europe country other than France and the French overseas territories (DROM-COM).
grants.amount: Funded through Culture Moves Europe: EUR 900 mobility allowance per person plus flat-rate travel coverage; supplements for green mobility, visa fees, dependent children under 18, OCT/OR residents, and disability/accessibility needs (including possible full coverage of a sign-language interpreter). On-site single-room accommodation, lunches at Le Milieu canteen (Tuesday to Friday), shared kitchen, and technical support provided. No application fee.
grants.description: AY-ROOP is launching an open call to select three circus artists and/or circus set designers/builders for a 21-day Culture Moves Europe research residency at Le Milieu (Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole) from 28 November to 18 December 2026. The residency is a research laboratory on reuse in contemporary circus: how everyday, locally available materials (paper/cardboard, plastics, lightweight metal, textiles, reclaimed wood, bicycle parts, etc.) can be used to create scenographic elements, sets, props and small apparatuses for juggling, acrobatics and balancing. The core principle is that objects are recreated locally from resources available in each territory rather than transported. AY-ROOP provides an equipped construction workshop, a rehearsal stage and circus tent, a guide to local reuse-sector contacts, and a full residency environment (accommodation, shared kitchen, working facilities); two mentors (a reuse specialist and a performing-arts professional) support the artists. PROFILES: circus artists interested in construction/scenography/DIY making, and builders/set designers working in circus; individual or group applications (max 3 people per group); emerging or established, any background. Accessible to artists with disabilities and accessibility needs. Working language English. No aerial apparatus or rigging: all work is ground-based. ELIGIBILITY: aged 18+ (no upper limit); legally resident in a Creative Europe country other than France and DROM-COM; available for the full residency. APPLY with a single PDF (1-page bio/CV, 5-page portfolio or video links, 1-page statement of intent, proof of residence). DEADLINE: 3 July 2026. Results announced 13 July 2026. Check the linked call PDF for full details.

grants.title: Circuit Breakers '26: Tech Worker Conference - Call for Proposals (New York City)
grants.location: New York City, USA. In-person conference 17-18 October 2026. Open to workers across the tech sector, from software engineers and salespeople to warehouse workers, ride-share drivers and contractors.
grants.amount: No funding. This is a free call for talk/session proposals at a volunteer-organised tech labour conference, not a grant. No submission fee.
grants.description: Circuit Breakers '26 is a tech worker conference organised by volunteers from across the tech labour movement, bringing together workers, organisers and activists in the tech industry to learn, build community and strategise. Tech Workers Coalition defines tech workers broadly, from software engineers and salespeople to warehouse workers and ride-share drivers. The conference centres rank-and-file organising, with workers sharing stories, running skills trainings and leading debates. The open call for proposals welcomes sessions on all aspects of labour organising in tech, with particular interest in three areas: Labor Organizing Against Imperialism; Tech Workers Taking on Technology (layoffs, surveillance, worker control over AI and automation); and Bold Strategy (solidarity, membership structures, return-to-office actions, cross-border organising). Sessions can be proposed via a short form (about 10 minutes). New York City, 17-18 October 2026. This is not a grant and carries no funding; it is a free call for conference talks. NOTE: the call-for-proposals deadline was not yet published at time of listing; confirm the submission deadline and form link on the Circuit Breakers page.

grants.title: Musikfonds: STIP-6 AI Scholarship Programme 2027 (Germany)
grants.location: Germany. Open to Germany-based professional freelance musicians; no computer-science background required.
grants.amount: EUR 30,000 lump sum per scholarship, paid in twelve monthly installments across January to December 2027. Ten scholarships available. No application fee.
grants.description: STIP-6 is a Musikfonds scholarship programme supporting professional musicians who work on AI-based composition and who reflect critically and societally on the role of artificial intelligence in music. Ten scholarships of EUR 30,000 each are awarded for the 2027 working year. Open to Germany-based professional freelance musicians across genres; no computer-science or technical background is required. Deadline 30 June 2026, 18:00 CET. Details and application via the Musikfonds funding programmes page (apply at musikfonds-antrag.de). NOTE: this entry was sourced from research and the funder's pages may update; confirm current figures and deadline before applying.

grants.title: Musikfonds: Small Project Funding (New and Experimental Music) 2026 (Germany)
grants.location: Germany. Applicants and projects based in Germany; priority to rural and structurally weaker regions.
grants.amount: Up to EUR 3,000 per project (minimum 10 percent co-financing; artist fee at least EUR 350 per concert). No application fee.
grants.description: Musikfonds Small Project Funding supports new, experimental and contemporary music projects with grants of up to EUR 3,000, prioritising projects in rural and structurally weaker regions of Germany. A minimum 10 percent co-financing is required and the artist fee must be at least EUR 350 per concert. Applicants and projects must be based in Germany. Application windows close 31 August 2026 and 30 November 2026. NOTE: research-sourced; confirm current figures and window dates on the Musikfonds site before applying.

grants.title: Musikfonds: Large Project Funding (New and Experimental Music) 2026 (Germany)
grants.location: Germany. Applicants and projects based in Germany; excludes pure audio/video productions, university and amateur-only projects.
grants.amount: Up to EUR 50,000 per project (minimum 10 percent co-financing; artist fee at least EUR 300 per concert). No application fee.
grants.description: Musikfonds Large Project Funding supports new, experimental and contemporary music projects with grants of up to EUR 50,000. A minimum 10 percent co-financing is required and the artist fee must be at least EUR 300 per concert. Applicants and projects must be based in Germany; pure audio or video productions, university projects and amateur-only projects are excluded. The application window runs 1 to 30 September 2026. NOTE: research-sourced; confirm current figures and window dates on the Musikfonds site before applying.

grants.title: MAK Center: Artists and Architects in Residence 2027/2028 (MAK Schindler Scholarship, Los Angeles)
grants.organization: MAK Center for Art and Architecture, Los Angeles
grants.location: Los Angeles, California, United States (Mackey Apartments). Residents must be present in L.A. for the full six-month term.
grants.amount: Six-month MAK Schindler Scholarship residency with accommodation provided at the Mackey Apartments. The open call does not state a stipend, travel or honorarium figure; confirm any additional financial support directly with the MAK Center. No application fee stated.
grants.duration: Six months. Residency terms run October to March or April to September; 2027/2028 cycle.
grants.description: The MAK Center for Art and Architecture awards Artists and Architects in Residence scholarships (the MAK Schindler Scholarship) to independent projects exploring the relation between art and architecture. Projects are chosen for their ability to critically examine current trends in art, architecture and society; to make a compelling connection with Los Angeles as the place of implementing or continuing the applicant's own work; and to demonstrate a conceptual and experimental approach. Emphasis is placed on the L.A. location, and residents' projects often reflect, incorporate and address specific facets of the city and its social fabric. Recipients are encouraged to take part in the MAK Center's activities and to attend meetings, workshops and project discussions with other artists and students. ELIGIBILITY: freelance artists; fine arts or architecture students at a university who have completed the '2. Studienabschnitt'; or architects or artists who have recently graduated. Teams of up to three are eligible if all members practice together as an artist group or collective of architects, are all present in L.A. for the six months, and name a contact responsible for the team. HOUSING: the Mackey Apartments are made available to scholarship holders only; family members, relatives and friends may be accommodated for a maximum of two weeks during the term, permitted in advance by Beth Stryker, Director of the MAK Center. Eligibility is excluded if the applicant has been or will be granted another scholarship in the USA immediately before or after the MAK scholarship, or if the data form is incomplete or the required portfolio is missing. Residency terms run October to March and April to September. Deadline 12 August 2026. Apply: https://www.makcenter.org/apply-mackey

grants.title: Project Liberty: Co-Lab Fund Member Collaboration Grants (Round 1, 2026)
grants.organization: Project Liberty (Alliance SteerCo); pooled fund supported by Future of Life Institute, Omidyar Network, Ergo Impact, and Project Liberty
grants.location: Members of the Project Liberty coalition (Alliance). Projects led by a Coalition member organization; geography otherwise unrestricted.
grants.amount: Grants cap at USD 250,000 per project; most first-round awards fall between USD 25,000 and USD 100,000. Pooled fund supported by Future of Life Institute, Omidyar Network, Ergo Impact, and Project Liberty. No application fee.
grants.duration: Project length varies. Funding rounds run monthly until December 2026, or until the funds are disbursed; decisions are expected within roughly three weeks of each deadline.
grants.description: MEMBERS ONLY: this is a member-led collaboration fund, not an open public call. Projects must be led by a Project Liberty Coalition (Alliance) member organization. The Co-Lab Fund, launched by the coalition's Steering Committee (SteerCo), supports projects that Coalition members undertake together, building on the trust and relationships that already exist within the community so that funding decisions can rest on the strength of the idea, the people behind it, and their track records rather than extensive diligence. In exchange for a streamlined process, grants stay modest and funded teams share their progress with the broader Coalition as projects develop, creating accountability and letting the community learn from what emerges. Two kinds of projects qualify: (1) work that advances the Coalition's active workstreams (listed in the application), and (2) investment in the Coalition's connective infrastructure, such as shared services and resources that strengthen collective capacity. Eligibility: projects must be led by a Coalition member organization, produce value for more than one Coalition organization, begin substantive work within a few weeks of approval, and fall within the active workstreams; full eligibility details are in the Overview and FAQ document. How it works: monthly rounds are planned until December 2026 or until the funds are disbursed; the application is designed to take an hour or less once the project's shape is clear, and decisions are expected within about three weeks of each deadline. This is the first cycle and the Fund is being built as it goes, so the process is expected to improve each round. Round 1 closes Sunday, 21 June 2026; further rounds follow, so there is no need to force an idea into this cycle. Apply via the Fillout application form.

grants.title: TechCongress: Congressional Innovation Fellowship 2027 (Senior and Early-Career)
grants.location: Washington, DC, United States. Full-time, in-person; remote participation is not possible. Nonpartisan: fellows serve in both Democratic and Republican offices.
grants.amount: Senior (mid-career) Fellows: USD 100,600/year stipend. Early-career Fellows: USD 78,000 annual-equivalent stipend (USD 6,500/month). Plus health insurance supplement up to USD 425/month, up to USD 2,000 relocation to DC, up to USD 2,000 first-month short-term housing reimbursement, up to USD 2,000 official travel, and a USD 500 professional-attire allowance. No application fee.
grants.duration: Senior Fellows serve January to December 2027 (optional one-month extension). Early-career Fellows serve January to October 2027.
grants.description: TechCongress places technologists in the US Congress to build technology capacity among Members and Congressional staff, where fewer than 3% of legislative staff have a technical background. For the January 2027 cohort it is recruiting up to twenty fellows (10 for Democratic offices, 10 for Republican offices) across two tiers: the Senior Congressional Innovation Fellowship (mid-career, at least eight years of work or postgraduate study) and the Congressional Innovation Fellowship (early-career, two to six years of experience, including a recently finished or projected-by-January-2027 graduate technical degree such as a Masters or PhD; applicants with seven years qualify for an intermediate tier). Fellows choose a placement with a Member of Congress or a Congressional Committee, report to a senior staffer (such as a Chief of Staff or Staff Director), and do work similar to other Congressional staff: researching tech policy (cyber and election security, data and biometric privacy, AI policy, autonomous vehicles, health IT, encryption, disinformation), educating Members and staff, writing legislation, preparing Committee hearings, markups and investigations, and building coalitions. No prior government experience is required; the fellowship is first and foremost an educational experience. This year TechCongress also seeks at least two fellows from non-traditional verticals: climate (grid modernization, clean energy, NEPA reform), government innovation (govtech and digital benefits delivery, civil service reform), and science, research and innovation (NSF, NIH, DoE, FDA reform, clinical trials). Technical degrees or experience in engineering, development, journalism, scientific, legal or government roles are encouraged. TechCongress is a nonpartisan nonprofit incubated at the Open Technology Institute at New America; since 2016 it has sent 140 fellows to Congress. Equal opportunity employer. Application deadline: 26 June 2026, 5:59am (applicant's local timezone). Contact: aleena@techcongress.io. Apply via the official application form.

grants.title: Genius Loci Weimar: Video Mapping Competition 2026 (Democracy and Society)
grants.location: Weimar, Germany. Open to international artists; winning works are projected onto historic facades during the festival.
grants.amount: EUR 7,000 production budget per winning facade (one winner selected per building) plus a EUR 3,000 festival prize for one selected work. No application fee.
grants.duration: Festival 11-13 September 2026; deliverable is a roughly 5-minute projection-mapping work for a facade.
grants.description: Genius Loci Weimar is an annual projection-mapping (video mapping) festival that invites artists to design audiovisual works for the facades of historic Weimar buildings. The 2026 competition theme is Democracy and Society. Entry is at concept stage: submissions are conceptual proposals, and professional video-mapping expertise is not required, as the organisers provide technical support to realise winning concepts. One winner is selected per facade and receives a EUR 7,000 production budget; one selected work additionally receives a EUR 3,000 festival prize. Open to international artists working as individuals or in teams. Winners are announced around 6 July 2026 and the festival runs 11-13 September 2026. Application deadline: 14 June 2026, 23:59 CET. Register and submit via the official competition page.

grants.title: Music WorX: Music-Tech Incubator 2026 (Hamburg)
grants.location: Hamburg, Germany. Open to European founders and founding teams; Hamburg-anchored program with a European mentor network.
grants.amount: EUR 3,200 development budget per team plus a EUR 1,500 per month living allowance across the 3-month programme. End-of-programme prizes: EUR 1,000 audience award and EUR 2,500 jury award. No equity is taken. No application fee.
grants.duration: 3-month programme, roughly early September to early December 2026.
grants.description: Music WorX is the music-technology incubator run by Hamburg Kreativ Gesellschaft for early-stage teams building products at the intersection of music and technology (creation tools, interactive media, streaming and monetisation, AI for music, instruments and audio gear). Selected teams receive a EUR 3,200 development budget for support services plus a EUR 1,500 per month living allowance over the 3-month programme, mentoring from a European music-tech network, and the chance to win a EUR 1,000 audience prize and a EUR 2,500 jury prize at demo day. The incubator takes no equity. Open to European founders and founding teams (teams of at least two recommended, solo founders welcome); no formal company formation is required to apply. The programme runs roughly September to December 2026. Application deadline: 17 June 2026. Apply via the Airtable form linked from the official programme page.

grants.title: EIC Accelerator 2026 (European Innovation Council)
grants.location: EU Member States and Horizon Europe Associated Countries. Single-company applicants; third-country applicants must relocate before the full-application stage.
grants.amount: Grant up to EUR 2.5 million (non-dilutive lump sum) for innovation activities, plus optional equity investment of EUR 1-10 million via the EIC Fund. Options: grant-only, blended finance, or investment-only. No application fee.
grants.duration: Project-based; supports development and scale-up at TRL 5/6 to 8.
grants.description: The EIC Accelerator funds high-risk, high-impact deep-tech SMEs and start-ups to develop and scale breakthrough innovations. It offers a grant of up to EUR 2.5 million (a non-dilutive lump sum) for innovation activities, and can combine this with an equity investment of EUR 1-10 million through the EIC Fund (blended finance), or provide investment only. The Step 1 short application can be submitted at any time; full (Step 2) applications are evaluated at fixed cut-offs. The next 2026 cut-offs are 8 July, 2 September and 4 November 2026 (all 17:00 Brussels time). Eligible applicants are for-profit SMEs and start-ups (fewer than 250 employees, turnover up to EUR 50M or balance sheet up to EUR 43M) established in an EU Member State or Horizon Europe Associated Country; small mid-caps up to 499 employees are eligible for investment-only support. Apply via the EU Funding and Tenders portal (topic HORIZON-EIC-2026-ACCELERATOR-01).

grants.title: Stimuleringsfonds: Digital Culture Grant Scheme 2026 (final round)
grants.organization: Stimuleringsfonds Creatieve Industrie (Creative Industries Fund NL)
grants.location: Netherlands. Requires Dutch Chamber of Commerce (KvK) registration.
grants.amount: EUR 10,000-25,000 for solo projects; EUR 10,000-50,000 for collaborative projects with partners. At least 20 percent of total project costs must be covered by co-financing. No application fee.
grants.description: The Stimuleringsfonds Digital Culture Grant Scheme (Deelregeling Digitale cultuur) supports projects by makers, researchers, design studios and cultural institutions positioned professionally in digital culture. Solo projects can receive EUR 10,000-25,000; collaborative projects with partners can receive up to EUR 50,000. At least 20 percent of total project costs must be covered by co-financing. Applicants must be registered with the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (KvK). The 2026 application window for this round runs 12-19 August 2026 (opens 12 August 15:00, closes 19 August 16:00 CEST); in 2026 the scheme runs only two rounds and this is the last. No application fee. Apply via the official scheme page.

grants.title: Stimuleringsfonds + Film Fund: Immerse\Interact 2026 (second round)
grants.organization: Stimuleringsfonds Creatieve Industrie and the Netherlands Film Fund
grants.location: Netherlands. Requires Dutch Chamber of Commerce (KvK) registration.
grants.amount: Development: EUR 15,000 for individual makers (plus up to EUR 5,000 coaching, max EUR 20,000) or EUR 25,000 for producers (plus up to EUR 5,000 coaching). Realisation: up to EUR 35,000 for individual makers or up to EUR 125,000 for producers (Fund covers max 85 percent). No application fee.
grants.description: Immerse\Interact is a joint grant scheme of the Stimuleringsfonds Creatieve Industrie and the Netherlands Film Fund for immersive and interactive media projects (VR, MR, AR and other interactive storytelling). It funds both development and realisation. Development support is EUR 15,000 for individual makers (plus up to EUR 5,000 coaching, capped at EUR 20,000) or EUR 25,000 for producers; realisation support is up to EUR 35,000 for individual makers or up to EUR 125,000 for producers (the Fund covers a maximum of 85 percent of costs). Open to professional makers, collectives and producers with a demonstrable track record in immersive or interactive media; applicants must be registered with the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (KvK). The second 2026 round runs 24 June to 25 August 2026 (opens 24 June 15:00, closes 25 August 16:00 CEST). No application fee. Questions: immerseinteract@stimuleringsfonds.nl.

grants.title: SAT x LAB7: Performing Arts and Technology Research-Creation Residency 2026 (Montreal)
grants.organization: Societe des arts technologiques (SAT) in partnership with LAB7 / 7 Fingers (Les 7 Doigts)
grants.location: Montreal, Canada. Open internationally; no nationality restriction stated.
grants.amount: CAD 10,000 grant per selected project, plus roughly two weeks (about 10 days) of studio workspace at the 7 Fingers Creation Centre and personalised coaching from the SAT and LAB7 teams. No application fee.
grants.duration: Around two weeks (10 days) of in-studio residency.
grants.description: The Societe des arts technologiques (SAT) and LAB7 (the research-creation lab of 7 Fingers / Les 7 Doigts) offer research-creation residencies at the intersection of performing arts and technology, on the theme of human movement. Each selected project receives a CAD 10,000 grant, roughly two weeks (about 10 days) of studio workspace at the 7 Fingers Creation Centre in Montreal, and personalised coaching from the SAT and LAB7 teams. Open to non-profit organisations, collectives and independent artists working between performing arts and technological arts; the call is effectively open internationally, with no nationality restriction stated. No application fee. Results are announced in early October 2026. Application deadline: 31 August 2026, 17:00. Apply via the official open-call page.

grants.title: Eurostars Call 11 2026 (Eureka / Eurostars-3)
grants.location: International consortia from at least two Eurostars countries, led by an innovative SME. Dutch funding administered by RVO.
grants.amount: Co-funds collaborative international R&D; national funding varies by country. For the Dutch project share: up to EUR 500,000 per project at 50 percent reimbursement of eligible R&D costs. No application fee.
grants.description: Eurostars (part of the Eureka network, the Eurostars-3 programme co-funded by Horizon Europe) supports collaborative international R&D projects led by innovative SMEs. Each country funds its own participants, so amounts vary; for the Dutch share, RVO funds up to EUR 500,000 per project at a 50 percent reimbursement rate on eligible R&D costs. Projects require an international consortium of at least two independent organisations from at least two Eurostars countries, led by an innovative SME, with the SME budget at least 50 percent of the total and no single participant exceeding 70 percent. Projects must run no longer than 36 months and be civil (non-defence) in purpose. Call 11 opens 9 July 2026 and closes 10 September 2026, 14:00 CEST. No application fee. Submit via the Eureka project platform (myeurekaproject.org); Dutch national information at RVO.

grants.title: MIT R&D Collaboration Projects 2026 (MKB Innovatiestimulering Topsectoren)
grants.location: Netherlands. SME consortia (minimum two collaborating SMEs).
grants.amount: Grant covering 35 percent of eligible costs. Small projects EUR 50,000-200,000; large projects EUR 200,000-350,000 per project (EUR 25,000-100,000 or EUR 25,000-175,000 per participant). No application fee.
grants.description: The MIT R&D Collaboration Projects scheme (MIT R&D-samenwerkingsprojecten, part of MKB Innovatiestimulering Topsectoren) funds joint R&D projects by Dutch SMEs within the national Top Sectors and Mission-driven Innovation policy. The grant covers 35 percent of eligible costs: small projects of EUR 50,000-200,000 and large projects of EUR 200,000-350,000 (with per-participant ranges of EUR 25,000-100,000 for small and EUR 25,000-175,000 for large). Eligible applicants are consortia of at least two collaborating SMEs, with no single participant carrying more than 70 percent of costs. The national RVO round runs 9 June to 15 September 2026 (closes 17:00) and is a ranked tender, not first-come-first-served; some provinces run separate regional MIT windows. No application fee. Apply via RVO.

grants.title: MoTA Museum of Transitory Art: Production Residency 2027 (Ljubljana)
grants.location: Ljubljana, Slovenia. Open internationally to artists of all nationalities and professional levels.
grants.amount: Full support including accommodation, technical support, production costs and an artist fee. The exact EUR artist-fee amount is not published; confirm by email. No application fee.
grants.duration: Production residency of up to one month.
grants.description: MoTA (Museum of Transitory Art) in Ljubljana runs an annual open call for production residencies for media, sound and visual artists, curators and researchers. Production residencies offer full support including accommodation, technical support, production costs and an artist fee; the exact artist-fee amount is not published on the official or aggregator pages, so applicants should confirm the figure directly by email. Residencies run up to one month. Open internationally: artists of all nationalities and at all professional levels are encouraged to apply, as individuals or collectives. No application fee. The annual deadline is 15 September (for residencies the following year). Apply by email to mota.artists@gmail.com with the subject line Application for production residency. Note: the official motamuseum.com SSL certificate is currently expired, which may briefly affect link reliability.

grants.title: Amarte Fonds: Development and Project Contributions (rolling)
grants.location: Netherlands and the Caribbean part of the Kingdom (the ABC and SSS islands). Applicants must be based there.
grants.amount: Development contribution up to EUR 2,750; project contribution up to EUR 5,500. Up to 80 percent paid in advance, remainder after the report is approved. No application fee.
grants.description: Amarte Fonds is a private Dutch foundation that supports the arts, with rolling (continuous) contributions for visual artists and independent curators. There are two contribution types: a development contribution of up to EUR 2,750 and a project contribution of up to EUR 5,500. Up to 80 percent is paid in advance, with the remainder after the final report is approved. Applicants must be based in the Netherlands or the Caribbean part of the Kingdom (the ABC and SSS islands). The fund also supports work across theatre, film, music, literature and interdisciplinary projects through specific programmes. There is no fixed deadline and no application fee; decisions are relatively fast. Apply year-round via the online portal.

grants.title: Abbey Road Red: Music-Tech Incubator (rolling, London)
grants.organization: Abbey Road Red (Abbey Road Studios / Universal Music Group)
grants.location: London, UK. Global applicants; selected startups are based in London during the 6-month programme.
grants.amount: In-kind incubator: access to Abbey Road and UMG studios, experts, facilities and the global label and publisher network, plus mentorship and some finance. Universal Music Group takes a 2 percent equity stake plus a participation right in future financing rounds. This is NOT a grant. No application fee.
grants.description: Abbey Road Red is the music-technology incubator run by Abbey Road Studios (part of Universal Music Group). It is primarily an in-kind and mentorship programme rather than a cash grant: selected early-stage music-tech startups (building tools to create, capture or connect music) get access to Abbey Road and UMG studios, experts and facilities, and the global label and publisher network, plus mentorship and some finance, over a 6-month programme. In exchange, Universal Music Group takes a 2 percent equity stake plus a participation right in future financing rounds. Open to startups worldwide; participants are based in London during the programme. Applications are rolling via the open form on the Abbey Road Red site. No application fee. Note: this is an equity-based incubator, not a grant.

grants.title: Yamaha Music Innovations Fund (rolling, equity investment)
grants.location: Silicon Valley, USA. Global reach; no stated geographic restriction.
grants.amount: USD 50 million venture fund making early-stage equity investments. This is an equity INVESTMENT, not a grant. No application fee.
grants.description: The Yamaha Music Innovations Fund is a USD 50 million venture fund (Yamaha Music Innovations Fund I, LP, managed by Yamaha Music Innovations, LLC) that makes early-stage equity investments in startups across four themes: creative tools and infrastructure; innovative and interactive media; community and collaboration; and empowerment and accessibility (including AI for music and video production, instruments and audio gear, and creator streaming and monetisation). It is an equity investment vehicle, not a grant. Based in Silicon Valley with global reach and no stated geographic restriction. Startups pitch on a rolling basis via the fund's Pitch Us form. No application fee.

grants.title: Sony Innovation Fund (rolling, equity investment)
grants.location: Global; offices in the US (Bay Area, Los Angeles), London/EU, Tokyo, Bangalore and Israel.
grants.amount: Corporate venture capital; typical check roughly USD 100,000 to USD 5 million (reported AUM USD 250 million plus), plus mentorship and access to Sony's network. This is an equity INVESTMENT, not a grant. No application fee.
grants.description: The Sony Innovation Fund (now under Sony Ventures Corporation) is Sony's corporate venture capital arm, investing in early-stage (Seed, Series A and Series B) transformational-technology startups across business, entertainment and society, including entertainment, gaming, AI, robotics, fintech, climate and blockchain. Music, entertainment and AI startups fit within its thesis, though it is not a music-only fund. Typical checks are roughly USD 100,000 to USD 5 million, alongside mentorship and access to Sony's network and R&D. It is an equity investment, not a grant. Global, with offices in the US, London/EU, Tokyo, Bangalore and Israel. Startups engage on a rolling basis via the contact and inquiry form. No application fee.

grants.title: UNIIQ: Proof-of-Concept Fund (rolling, Zuid-Holland)
grants.location: Zuid-Holland (South Holland), Netherlands only. Early-stage research-driven startups.
grants.amount: Around EUR 300,000 per company as a convertible loan (investment, not a grant). Total fund EUR 50 million. No application fee.
grants.duration: Investment via convertible loan; rolling intake.
grants.description: UNIIQ is a proof-of-concept investment fund for the earliest, riskiest phase of company building in Zuid-Holland (South Holland). It invests around EUR 300,000 per company as a convertible loan (for example redeemed over about three years or converted at a follow-on valuation), drawn from a EUR 50 million fund. It is an investment, not a grant. Eligible companies are early-stage, research-driven startups based in Zuid-Holland, typically in life sciences, deep tech and high tech. Intake is continuous via an orientation meeting followed by a funding application. No application fee.

grants.title: ROM InWest: Startup and Scale-up Financing (rolling, Noord-Holland)
grants.organization: ROM InWest (Regional Development Company for Noord-Holland)
grants.location: Noord-Holland (North Holland), Netherlands. Startups, scale-ups and innovative SMEs.
grants.amount: Financing of EUR 300,000 to EUR 5,000,000 as loans, equity participations or a combination (not a gift); cannot exceed 49.9 percent of a financing round. This is investment, not a grant. No application fee.
grants.description: ROM InWest is the regional development company for Noord-Holland (North Holland). It finances startups, scale-ups and innovative SMEs working on food, energy, healthcare and circular-economy transitions, providing EUR 300,000 to EUR 5,000,000 as loans, equity participations or a combination (explicitly not a gift), and not exceeding 49.9 percent of a financing round. It is an investment, not a grant. Companies must be based in Noord-Holland. Intake is continuous, starting with a quick-scan tool. No application fee.

grants.title: WBSO: R&D Tax Credit (rolling, Netherlands)
grants.location: Netherlands. Dutch companies and self-employed paying payroll or income tax that conduct qualifying R&D.
grants.amount: Payroll/income tax reduction for R&D, not a cash grant. 2026: 36 percent of the first EUR 380,000 of R&D wage costs (50 percent for startups), 16 percent above; fixed deduction for self-employed. National budget EUR 1.817 billion for 2026. No application fee.
grants.duration: Applied for in advance per period; cannot start before approval.
grants.description: The WBSO (Wet Bevordering Speur- en Ontwikkelingswerk) is the Dutch R&D tax credit administered by RVO. It reduces payroll/wage tax for companies with R&D staff and provides an income-tax deduction for self-employed people doing R&D; it is a tax credit, not a cash grant. For 2026 the benefit is 36 percent of the first EUR 380,000 of R&D wage and other costs (50 percent for qualifying startups) and 16 percent above that; self-employed receive a fixed deduction (and need at least 500 R&D hours per year). Eligible applicants are Dutch companies and self-employed people paying payroll or income tax who carry out qualifying R&D in the EU. Applications are made in advance via Mijn RVO and cannot be retroactive; for coverage of the final three months of a year the deadline is 30 September. Time-keeping and project administration are required. No application fee.

grants.title: Cultuur+Ondernemen: Cultuurlening (rolling loan, Netherlands)
grants.location: Netherlands. Creative and cultural professionals and organisations residing and working in NL.
grants.amount: Loan (must be repaid), up to EUR 500,000. Interest 2 percent up to EUR 50,000, then 2-5 percent (risk-based) above EUR 50,000; term up to 10 years. This is a LOAN, not a grant. No application fee.
grants.duration: Loan term up to 10 years; rolling.
grants.description: The Cultuurlening from Cultuur+Ondernemen is a low-interest loan (not a grant) for creative and cultural professionals and organisations in the Netherlands, to invest in their practice or business. Loans run up to EUR 500,000, with interest of 2 percent up to EUR 50,000 and 2-5 percent (risk-based) above EUR 50,000, and a term of up to 10 years. The loan must be repaid. Eligible applicants reside and work in the Netherlands and are either art-school graduates or have at least three years of professional practice (others can apply with a motivation and reference). Applications are accepted on a rolling basis with no fixed deadline. No application fee. Note: separate regional products exist (for example the Rotterdam and Amsterdam Cultuurlening) with different rates and caps.

grants.title: TBA21-Academy: Tentacular Fellowship 2026-2027 (Ocean Space, Venice)
