WHAT IS PROVIDED: free studio space; access to facilities including risograph printer, darkroom, ceramics studio, large-format printer, 3D printers, thermobinder, and laser cutter; biweekly studio visits with curators, gallerists, critics, and artists; free access to all public programmes at Pioneer Works, including Second Sundays (monthly open studios and performances). FINANCIAL AWARD: USD 5,000 for Visual Arts residents; USD 3,000 for Music residents (each comprising a USD 2,500 honorarium plus USD 500/month for the residency duration); applicants' choice on how to spend it. Pioneer Works does not provide housing, travel, or visa sponsorship. ELIGIBILITY: open locally, nationally, and internationally; collectives may apply (resources shared); current students and programme alumni are not eligible. REVIEW PROCESS: phase one is internal scoring on technical ability, uniqueness of vision, and future potential (nontraditional career paths given special consideration); highest scorers advance to an external jury of art professionals and alumni who assess diversity of demographics and media, genuine studio need, and fit with Pioneer Works' public-facing culture. Jury convenes September 2026; all applicants notified by late September. Supported by Dalio Philanthropies, the Andy Warhol Foundation, NYC Department of Cultural Affairs, New York State Council on the Arts, and the National Endowment for the Arts. APPLY: via Pioneer Works website by 13 July 2026. Questions: residency@pioneerworks.org.

grants.title: HHMI Presidential Fellows Program (Chevy Chase, MD)
grants.location: HHMI Headquarters, Chevy Chase, MD, USA (primarily on-site)
grants.amount: $150,000–$200,000/year salary, plus exceptional health benefits, retirement plans, time off, and recognition/wellness programs. This is a paid employment position, not a grant.
grants.description: HHMI places 2–3 Presidential Fellows per year directly in the Office of the President, working on the institute's highest-priority strategic initiatives. Fellows report to the President and the VP/COO/Senior Advisor. This is a high-trust, high-access, high-output role — not a rotation or observation post. WHAT FELLOWS DO: lead cross-functional strategic projects spanning science programs, AI, finance, communications, HR, technology, science education, and film/television; take initiatives from concept through implementation; manage AI agents and LLM-based workflows; handle sensitive and confidential matters; dedicate 10% of time to self-directed projects (subject to President/COO approval). CONTEXT: HHMI is one of the largest private funders of biomedical research in the world; it also runs national programs in science education, film and television, and AI-enabled research. This fellowship offers rare access to the intersection of science, philanthropy, education, media, and technology. ELIGIBILITY: bachelor's degree required; 0–5 years of professional experience in consulting, policy, research, strategy, law, operations, or comparable fields; welcomes recent graduates and those with advanced degrees (MBA, MPP, PhD, JD); must be fluent with AI tools and LLMs including experience orchestrating AI agents; exceptional written/verbal communication skills; comfort with ambiguity and executive pace. US-based (Chevy Chase, MD, primarily on-site). COMPENSATION: $150,000–$200,000/year. No application fee. APPLY via the ScienceCareers job posting.

grants.title: The Lupine Collaborative: 'Blackness, Queerness, and Nature' Anthology Call
grants.location: Remote / worldwide (diaspora)
grants.amount: $1,000 per accepted submission. No submission fee.
grants.description: NOTE: The final submission deadline of 1 March 2026 has passed. The entry is retained for reference. The Lupine Collaborative, in partnership with Milkweed Editions, sought submissions for an anthology co-edited by Grace Anderson and Erin Sharkey under the working title 'Blackness, Queerness, and Nature.' ELIGIBILITY: applicants must identify as both Black and Queer; open to authors from across the diaspora. ACCEPTED FORMS: personal and lyric essays, fiction, poems, photographs, oral histories, meditations, recipes, songs, stories, experimental writing, and other mediums (inquire). Maximum 5,000 words for longer pieces. COMPENSATION: $1,000 per accepted piece. THEMES: organised around cycles — Seeding (history/ancestry/traditional knowledge/precolonial history), Rooting (healing practices/alternative economies/Afro-Indigeneity), Fallowing (rest/grief/spirituality), Compost (grief/mourning/endings), and Harvesting (abolition/speculative/visions). Publication timeline: mid-to-late 2027. Milkweed Editions (Minneapolis) is a nonprofit independent publisher. Questions: submissions@thelupinecollaborative.org.

grants.title: The Cooper Prize 2026 (16th Street Foundation and Company 16, Melbourne)
grants.location: Melbourne, Australia (international eligibility)
grants.amount: AUD 20,000 prize to winner, plus consideration for a full commission and production of the winning work. All finalists receive professionally directed public readings by professional actors. No application fee.
grants.duration: Prize (one-time award); winning work may proceed to full commission and production.
grants.description: The Cooper Prize 2026 is an international playwriting prize administered by 16th Street Foundation and Company 16 in Melbourne, Australia. AUD 20,000 is awarded to the winning playwright, plus consideration for a full commission and professional production. All finalists receive professionally directed public readings by professional actors. Open internationally to published and unpublished playwrights at any career stage with no geographic restrictions. Submissions must respond to thematic prompts drawn from Victor Frankl's 'Man's Search for Meaning' and Dostoevsky's 'The Brothers Karamazov', exploring themes including doubt versus faith, the meaning of suffering, and the power of truth. Application requires: (1) a one-page document covering motivation for writing, issues to explore, philosophical perspective or worldview, and a synopsis or treatment; (2) ten pages of the play manuscript. Judging panel led by Iain Sinclair and Harry Haynes. Deadline: 19 June 2026. Apply via JotForm at https://form.jotform.com/260408855140858.

grants.title: ROTLICHT Festival for Analog Photography 2026: Open Call (Vienna, Austria)
grants.location: Academy of Fine Arts, Vienna, Austria
grants.amount: EUR 250 cash award per selected winner + EUR 150 travel allowance. Grand Prix winner receives an additional EUR 250, a solo exhibition, and a free hotel stay. Submission fee: EUR 20 per project.
grants.duration: Exhibition at Academy of Fine Arts Vienna; announcement August 2026, public reveal September 2026.
grants.description: ROTLICHT Festival for Analog Photography 2026, organised by Zigutamve – Association for Contemporary Photography, selects 20 projects for exhibition at the Academy of Fine Arts Vienna. Theme: 'Anthropocene: Hybrid Realities' – exploring the tension between human ecological dominance and environmental fragility. Each selected winner receives: exhibition in a 1,300+ m² venue, publication in the official festival catalogue (2 copies), EUR 250 cash award, EUR 150 travel allowance, festival pass, and free registration for Photodays Rovinj 2027. Grand Prix winner additionally receives a solo exhibition, an extra EUR 250, a free hotel stay, and a catalogue cover feature. ELIGIBILITY: open to any individual aged 16 or older, at any career stage, internationally; submissions must include at least one analog photographic process; works must have been taken within the last 5 years (2021-2026); no AI-generated or AI-enhanced work (unless deliberately thematic). Files must be JPEG, max 5 MB, minimum 2000px on longest side, 300 dpi. Jury selects winners by late July 2026. Deadline: 30 June 2026. NOTE: EUR 20 submission fee per project applies. Apply at https://www.rotlicht-festival.at/open-call-submission/.

grants.title: Prix Ibn Khaldoun – Léopold Sédar Senghor Translation Prize 2026 (OIF / ALECSO)
grants.organization: Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF) and Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO)
grants.location: International (Francophone zone and Arab world)
grants.amount: EUR 10,000 (funded equally by OIF and ALECSO). No application fee.
grants.description: The Ibn Khaldoun – Léopold Sédar Senghor Translation Prize (19th edition, 2026) is awarded jointly by the Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF) and ALECSO to recognise excellence in literary and humanities/social sciences translation between French and Arabic in either direction. Cash award: EUR 10,000. ELIGIBILITY: open to individual translators, universities and higher education institutions, research centres, national associations and unions, and publishing houses from the Arab world and Francophone space. Works must be literary or humanities/social sciences texts translated between French and Arabic (either direction), published within 3 years prior to the application year, and must be a first translation of the original. Self-published or photocopied works excluded. One work maximum per candidate. Applications submitted exclusively electronically; required materials: CV, presentation of translation work, copies of both translated and original texts. Deadline: 30 June 2026. Apply at https://appelsaprojets.francophonie.org/fr/applications/soutien-a-la-circulation-des-biens-et-mobilite-des-artistes.

grants.title: Siirtymä Ambient Music Festival 2026: Open Call (Tampere, Finland)
grants.location: Tampere, Finland (multiple simultaneous venues, site-specific)
grants.amount: Technical support provided. No artist fee publicly confirmed. No application fee.
grants.description: Siirtymä Ambient Music Festival 2026 is a free, community-driven festival in Tampere, Finland, organised by Suomen Ambientyhdistys ry (Finnish Ambient Association). The festival uses overlapping simultaneous performances across multiple locations, emphasising spatial and ambient experience over traditional stage formats. Open to artists and collectives working in ambient music, sound art, spatial installations, and interdisciplinary practices; no career-stage restriction; the festival prioritises emerging and lesser-known artists. The 2026 theme focuses on strengthening diversity, creating multiple voices, and finding new ways to encounter one another and the urban environment. Technical support is provided to selected participants. No travel or accommodation funding is publicly confirmed. NOTE: deadline of June 30 is from the Community Arts Network PDF; verify at https://siirtyma.com/en/siirtym-2026 before submitting. Contact Suomen Ambientyhdistys ry directly for application details.

grants.title: Women Photograph + Leica Project Grants 2026 (Documentary Photography)
grants.location: Remote (international)
grants.amount: Three grants of USD 10,000 each (USD 30,000 total). No application fee.
grants.description: Women Photograph + Leica Project Grants 2026 awards three grants of USD 10,000 each to support documentary photography projects. ELIGIBILITY: open to women, nonbinary, transgender, and intersex documentary photographers of any nationality; no membership required; no restrictions on camera equipment or career stage. Funding covers hard costs of reporting, photographers' creative fees, and any other expenses that support the production of new or in-progress still photography projects. Not intended for books, exhibitions, or workshops. Grantees retain full work ownership. One submission per applicant; applications in English (Google Translate acceptable); any use of generative AI must be disclosed. Applications opened 20 May 2026; deadline 30 June 2026 at 11:59 PM ET. Apply at https://app.picter.com/contests/bad7ea97-7b0d-4a9c-9e85-38e73529d62b/start.

grants.title: Nakanojo Biennale 2027: International Artist Open Call (Gunma Prefecture, Japan)
grants.organization: Nakanojo Biennale Executive Committee / Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture
grants.location: Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture, Japan
grants.amount: Production grant provided (amount not specified publicly). No exhibition fee, no residency fee. Insurance subsidies available. Artists responsible for materials, food, transportation, and installation/removal costs. No application fee.
grants.duration: Exhibition: 18 September – 24 October 2027 (37 days). Production period: April–August 2027. Residency accommodation available from April 2027.
grants.description: Nakanojo Biennale 2027 (11th edition) is an international open call for artists of all genres — sculpture, painting, photography, video art, installation, performance, and more — to exhibit in Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Theme: 'Echoing Green', inspired by William Blake's poem, exploring cycles of life, regeneration, and connection between past and future generations. Open internationally to individual artists and artist groups at all career stages; new participants are encouraged. A production grant is provided (amount not publicly specified); no exhibition or residency fees are charged. Residency accommodation available from April 2027. Staff support provided for venue selection and installation guidance. First-time participants in Japan must attend an on-site venue inspection; an online orientation on 5 September 2026 is required for all accepted artists. Deadline: 12 July 2026 at 23:59 JST (online). Apply via Google Form at https://forms.gle/SS8DaaGA6uwUKogj7. Full details at https://nakanojo-biennale.com/2027-open-call/english.html. Contact: office@nakanojo-biennale.com.

grants.title: Unruly Mirrors Open Call 2026 (East and Central Asia, Feminist Art)
grants.location: East and Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Japan, South Korea, Mainland China, Hong Kong, Uzbekistan, Mongolia)
grants.amount: Financial support details not publicly confirmed at time of entry. No application fee confirmed.
grants.description: Unruly Mirrors is an open call for artists based in Kazakhstan, Japan, South Korea, Mainland China, Hong Kong, Uzbekistan, or Mongolia. The project examines beauty practices in East and Central Asia as spaces of norm reproduction, intimacy, and feminist resistance. Financial support and full eligibility criteria were not publicly confirmed at time of entry (the website was unreachable). Deadline: 15 July 2026. Apply or inquire at https://unrulymirrors.com. NOTE: verify financial support and full details directly before applying.

grants.title: OIF: Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods 2026 (Francophonie)
grants.location: Francophone zone (OIF member states globally)
grants.amount: Up to EUR 5,000 for artist mobility (travel, accommodation, visas, logistics). Up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs). Both can be combined. No application fee.
grants.duration: Residencies or tours of 5 days to 3 months. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026.
grants.description: The OIF Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods programme provides grants of up to EUR 5,000 for mobility (travel, accommodation, visa fees, logistics) and up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs) within the Francophone zone; both can be combined. ELIGIBILITY: professional artists and established artistic collectives with a demonstrated connection to the Francophone space (by nationality, working language, or artistic collaborations), residing in an OIF member state, with minimum 2 years of experience in the cultural or artistic sector; must directly manage the project (not act as intermediaries); one application per individual per call. ELIGIBLE DISCIPLINES: live performance (music, theatre, dance, comedy, storytelling), cinema/audiovisual, literature/publishing, and visual arts. PRIORITY given to projects promoting gender equality, women artists, creators under 35, and South-South mobility. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026. Multiple selection sessions throughout the year; call open until 31 August 2026. Information webinars: 11 March, 13 May, and 8 July 2026 at 3 PM Paris time. Contact: culture.dlc@francophonie.org. Apply at https://appelsaprojets.francophonie.org/fr/applications/soutien-a-la-circulation-des-biens-et-mobilite-des-artistes.

grants.title: Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships 2026 (Europe–Japan Exchange)
grants.location: Europe or Japan (bilateral exchange; fellows arrange own host institution)
grants.amount: Up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays). No application fee.
grants.duration: Minimum 3 months, maximum 1 year. Fellowships for 2027 start.
grants.description: Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships support bilateral research exchange between Europe and Japan, awarding up to 15 fellowships annually. AMOUNT: up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays), flexible — covers living costs, travel, insurance, research expenses, and books; fellows determine their own allocation. ELIGIBILITY: European nationals (including UK, Israel, Switzerland, Turkey, Balkan and Baltic countries) conducting research in Japan; or Japanese nationals conducting research in Europe. Applicants must have completed at least a Master's or PhD degree within the last 10 years (older qualifications may apply with supporting documentation). No restrictions on research field. Priority given to first-time applicants to Europe/Japan. Fellows arrange their own host institution and advisor. Up to 15 fellowships awarded annually. Deadline: 15 September 2026, 23:59 CET (for fellowships starting January-December 2027). Apply at https://portal.canonfoundation.org/. Contact: canon.foundation@canon-europe.com.

grants.title: NY20+ International Artist Residency (Chengdu, Sichuan, China, Ongoing)
grants.location: Chengdu, Sichuan, China
grants.amount: No participation fee. Host covers accommodation and project materials. Artists pay their own travel and personal expenses. No application fee.
grants.duration: Minimum 7 days; duration based on project plan.
grants.description: NY20+ International Artist Residency is an ongoing open residency in Chengdu, Sichuan, China, supported by Nongyuan Culture (founded 2008). Open internationally to artists, designers, art students, craftspeople, and groups from any discipline and at any career stage worldwide. The programme focuses on exploring Bashu Culture and Chengdu city, integrating traditional and modern elements, traditional Chinese handicrafts, and local heritage. PROVIDED: studio or workspace (private or shared) with basic art materials; furnished private or shared bedroom accommodation; meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner); technical support; cultural exchange opportunities; workshops, seminars, and exhibition or display opportunities. Artists are expected to donate one work to the residency upon completion. No fixed deadline; contact the organisation to confirm residency dates. Apply via the application form at http://www.ny20plus.com/en/h-col-158.html. Contact: ny20@cdnongyuan.com or +86 134 0287 6539.

grants.title: BFI Doc Society: Expanded Screen Fund (UK, VR/AR/Immersive Non-Fiction)
grants.location: UK (projects must demonstrate UK-wide benefit)
grants.amount: £25,000–£150,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.
grants.duration: Project-based; feature-length or series immersive non-fiction works
grants.description: The BFI Doc Society Expanded Screen Fund supports ambitious screen-based works of non-fiction intended to reach audiences beyond cinemas, including VR, AR, MR, and 360-degree experiences. Projects must be grounded in real events, locations, or narratives and must demonstrate a clear creative rationale for their technical approach. AMOUNT: £25,000–£150,000 non-recoupable grant. ELIGIBILITY: producers with a demonstrated track record in delivering immersive work; UK-based; projects must meet at least three of six stated priorities: equity/diversity/inclusion, audience impact, talent development, creative risk-taking, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. DEADLINE: 22 June 2026 at 1pm UK time. APPLY via the BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

grants.title: BFI Doc Society: Made of Truth – Short Non-Fiction Film Fund (UK)
grants.location: UK (both director and producer must be UK residents)
grants.amount: Up to £25,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.
grants.duration: Short films 5–40 minutes; up to 13 projects funded per year
grants.description: BFI Doc Society's Made of Truth fund supports early-career UK filmmakers making non-fiction live-action or animated short films of 5–40 minutes. Up to 13 projects are funded per annual round. AMOUNT: up to £25,000 per project. ELIGIBILITY: early-career directors and producers who are UK residents; teams must have at least two people (a producer and a director); directors cannot have received a commercial distribution deal for a non-fiction feature in the last five years; a reasonable proportion of early-career crew is required. Applications must meet at least three of six priorities: equity/diversity/inclusion, audience impact, talent development, creative risk, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. OPENS: 18 May 2026 at 1pm UK time. DEADLINE: 1 July 2026 at 1pm UK time. APPLY via the BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

grants.title: BFI Doc Society: Features Production Fund (UK, Documentary)
grants.location: UK
grants.amount: Up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.
grants.duration: Feature-length non-fiction films for theatrical release; rolling two-stage process (EOI then full application)
grants.description: The BFI Doc Society Features Production Fund (delivered via BFI with Doc Society as delegate partner) supports UK filmmakers producing non-fiction feature films intended for theatrical release. Two strands: Directions (first- or second-time directors) and Momentum (established talent). AMOUNT: up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant (the total Doc Society contribution per project cannot exceed £150,000). ELIGIBILITY: UK-based filmmakers, both emerging and established directors; experienced and debut producers eligible; projects must meet at least three of six stated priorities: equity/diversity, audience impact, talent development, creative risk, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. PROCESS: two-stage — submit an Expression of Interest, then a Full Application if invited. Rolling intake year-round. Opened May 2026. APPLY via BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

grants.title: Kometa Fellows 2026: Field Journalism, Photography, and Writing
grants.location: Remote / field-based (international)
grants.amount: €10,000 per Fellow, six Fellows selected. No application fee.
grants.duration: One year of funding for field work and research
grants.description: Kometa, a French literary and journalism magazine, awards fellowships to journalists, writers, and photographers documenting reality in contexts of war, censorship, or severe social and political tensions. Projects must be rooted in direct field experience and focused on contemporary rather than historical events. Projects already under a contractual agreement are ineligible. AMOUNT: €10,000 per Fellow, six Fellows selected. WHAT FELLOWS RECEIVE: one year of funding; publication of the completed project (partial or full) in Kometa; opportunity to contribute to the monthly newsletter; mentorship through editorial collaboration. 2026 FOCUS AREAS: disinformation and narrative wars; political and social changes in the Gulf. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. APPLY via the Tally form (https://tally.so/r/J9XxMK), in French or English (about 30 to 45 minutes). Contact: fellows@kometarevue.com.

grants.title: Simone Veil Prize for European Jewish Cultural Heritage 2026
grants.organization: European Commission, Directorate-General for Education, Youth, Sport and Culture
grants.location: EU Member States and third countries associated to the Creative Europe programme
grants.amount: €10,000 per Grand Prix winner (five winners, one per category); up to 25 finalists recognized. No application fee.
grants.duration: For projects completed between January 2023 and January 2026
grants.description: The Simone Veil Prize, administered by the European Commission, recognises outstanding projects preserving and promoting European Jewish cultural heritage. It is awarded across five categories: Shared Histories, Heritage for Dialogue, Living Heritage, Cross-border Cooperation and Shared Narratives, and Next Generation/Youth Engagement and Education. AWARD: €10,000 per Grand Prix winner (one per category, five in total); up to 25 finalists receive recognition. ELIGIBILITY: legal or natural persons (individuals or organisations) registered in EU Member States or third countries associated to the Creative Europe programme; projects must have been completed between January 2023 and January 2026. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026 at 23:59 CET. APPLY by submitting the completed application form by email to applications@simoneveilprize.eu.

grants.title: Lee Ufan Arles: Prix Art & Environnement 2026 (Residency + Exhibition Prize)
grants.organization: Lee Ufan Arles and Maison Guerlain
grants.location: Arles, France (residency and exhibition)
grants.amount: Funded residency (production space + accommodation) in Arles. No cash prize amount specified. No application fee.
grants.duration: 6–8 weeks residency in early 2027, followed by a solo summer 2027 exhibition
grants.description: The Prix Art & Environnement (4th edition), awarded by Lee Ufan Arles and Maison Guerlain, supports an artist working at the intersection of art and the natural environment. The prize winner receives a funded 6–8 week production residency in Arles in early 2027, including production space, accommodation, and mentorship from Lee Ufan Arles staff, culminating in a solo summer 2027 exhibition. ELIGIBILITY: open to all artists of any discipline and nationality, with no age or degree requirements; candidates must demonstrate at least one solo exhibition and one or more group exhibitions. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. APPLY via the Google Form linked from the Lee Ufan Arles website.

grants.title: Visual Anthropology Center (VAC): Residency Programme (Belgrade)
grants.location: Belgrade, Serbia (accommodation and studio in central Belgrade; additional workspace at Community Center KROV)
grants.amount: EUR 1,000–1,500 per month (stipend, varies by project requirements), plus accommodation and studio space provided. No application fee.
grants.duration: Approximately 1–2 months; flexible packages depending on project needs
grants.description: Visual Anthropology Center (VAC) in Belgrade invites filmmakers, visual anthropologists, artists, and researchers to apply for its residency programme. The residency specialises in visual anthropology but is also open to individual and collaborative projects in experimental ethnography, creative documentary film, and other artistic mediums. Residents may apply with a project aimed at a public showing, or use the time and space to reflect, research, or write without a specific outcome. WHAT IS PROVIDED: accommodation and studio space in central Belgrade; additional workspace and community connection through Community Center KROV (an independent creative hub hosting lectures, events, and exhibitions); project guidance and mentorship from film professionals, ethnomusicologists, producers, artists, curators, and anthropologists; connections to local cultural and academic institutions, archives, galleries, and exhibition spaces; access to camera equipment, translators for fieldwork or editing, fixers, and project-specific services or materials; opportunity to exhibit a final project as an exhibition, installation, screening, workshop, or lecture. Eligible projects may be promoted through VAC's platforms and partner networks, including the Vizantrop Film Festival, for regional and international visibility. ELIGIBILITY: filmmakers, visual anthropologists, artists, and researchers; individual and collaborative projects welcome. Applications accepted on a rolling basis.

grants.title: Iméra: 'This Is Not Another Fellowship' – Postdoctoral Residency 2026-27 (Aix-Marseille)
grants.organization: Iméra – Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université
grants.location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)
grants.amount: Free accommodation in campus apartment; travel expenses covered; office in co-working space. No cash stipend specified. No application fee.
grants.duration: 3 months: either November 2, 2026 – January 26, 2027, or March 1, 2027 – May 28, 2027.
grants.description: Iméra's 'This Is Not Another Fellowship' is a 3-month postdoctoral residency at the Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université, offered in two semester options: November 2, 2026 – January 26, 2027, or March 1, 2027 – May 28, 2027. PROVIDED: free accommodation in one of fourteen campus apartments; travel expenses covered; desk in shared open co-working space; participation in the weekly Community Building Seminar (mandatory 3-hour Thursday sessions); access to interdisciplinary scholarly networks. ELIGIBILITY: under 35 years old by May 28, 2027; currently holding a postdoctoral fellowship contract at a university; have not lived in France for more than 12 months in the prior 3 years; if requiring a visa, must demonstrate sufficient funds. Research must align with one of four Iméra programs: Arts & Sciences: Indisciplined Knowledge; Interdisciplinary Dialogues; Mediterranean; or Necessary Utopias. All disciplines welcome. DEADLINE: 3 July 2026 at 1pm (CET+2). Apply at https://www.imera.fr/en/this-is-not-another-fellowship-application-2026-27/.

grants.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
grants.organization: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission
grants.location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)
grants.amount: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.
grants.duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).
grants.description: The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

grants.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Grants for Applied Work 2026
grants.location: Worldwide / remote (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)
grants.amount: Typical grants $50,000–$2,000,000 per project; proposals outside this range considered when the work justifies it. May include some operational support for new organisations. No application fee.
grants.description: Longview Philanthropy invites proposals for applied work on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, or experiences of AI systems. Typical grants are $50,000–$2,000,000 per project, with proposals outside that range considered when justified. New organisations may also receive some operational support. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: seed funding for new organisations (think tanks, research bodies, initiatives); mentorship programmes for researchers entering the digital minds field; internships, new hires, or focused programmes on digital minds at existing AI organisations, academic labs, or policy institutions; convenings and field-building (workshops, conferences, summits, working groups addressing technical and governance questions). AREAS OF INTEREST: measuring AI welfare; consciousness and sentience research; governance and legal/social science frameworks for potentially sentient AI; communications work for thoughtful public discourse; research beyond the US; support organisations and operations professionals for independent technical researchers. SELECTION CRITERIA: potential impact, track record, demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relationship to AI risk, cost-effectiveness, legal compliance. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

grants.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Research Fellowships 2026
grants.location: Worldwide / remote or institutional (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)
grants.amount: $55,000–$120,000/year through a university, or $80,000–$150,000/year direct stipend; plus research budget (10% of salary), conference travel, and compute resources where applicable. No application fee.
grants.duration: 1–2 years. Second year conditional on continuing to meet programme requirements.
grants.description: Longview Philanthropy Research Fellowships support scholars pursuing research on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, and legal position of AI systems. AMOUNT: $55,000–$120,000/year (through a university, with 10% research budget and standard fringe benefits), or $80,000–$150,000/year (direct stipend, with suggested 5–10% allocation for research and travel). Compute resources available for machine learning research. Invitation and funding to attend the annual Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy Summit at NYU. ELIGIBILITY: must have or be expected to receive a doctoral degree in computer science, neuroscience, a related technical discipline, law, medicine, or applied social science by September 2027 or fellowship start date (exceptions for equivalent research experience); fellows arrange their own institutional affiliation; fellowship may be conditional on institutional affiliation or attendance at events. SELECTION CRITERIA: demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relation to AI risk; evidence of research promise (publications, academic achievements, references). DURATION: 1–2 years; second year conditional on ongoing programme requirements. AREAS OF INTEREST: introspection, welfare measurement, interpretability, model character, correlates of consciousness, valence research, governance frameworks, legal and political responses to potentially sentient AI. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Required application materials: CV, description of possible research directions (~1,000 words), intended outcomes, what you would do without this fellowship (~150–400 words), career goals (~300 words). Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

grants.title: WSW Artist's Book Residency Grant
grants.location: Rosendale, NY, USA; in-person residency.
grants.amount: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. No application fee.
grants.duration: 8-10 weeks; residency runs September through June, one year after application.
grants.description: Women's Studio Workshop (WSW) offers an eight- to ten-week residency for artists to produce a limited-edition artist's book. Working intensively in WSW's studios in Rosendale, NY, selected artists print and bind their own books; edition size must be between 50 and 100 copies. AMOUNT: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. WSW provides technical advice, training on new equipment, techniques, and materials, and production assistance. SALES CONTRACT: 20% of the edition goes to the artist; 20% (at least nine copies) stays with WSW for archival and institutional sales; 60% goes to general distribution and sale. If the artist sells through WSW, WSW takes a 30% commission plus 20% for marketing; the artist receives a 50% commission, paid annually around 30 June. WSW holds the right to sell distribution copies at a 20% discount to standing-order and repository institutions. WSW's books are held in over 450 collections worldwide, including the Library of Congress, Yale University, and Vassar College. An outside jury of artists and curators selects recipients. INDIGENOUS ARTISTS: applicants may check the appropriate box on the Submittable form to be considered for the BIMA Artist's Book Residency Grant for Indigenous Artists. APPLICATION MATERIALS (upload via Submittable): current resume; brief project description including media required (200-word maximum); materials budget form (Google Sheets, Excel, or PDF); up to ten images of recent work; image script (title, medium, dimensions, date). MAILED MATERIALS (to WSW at the address below): a physical book mock-up demonstrating how the book will be bound or function, actual size, with at least 1-2 fully realised spreads; and the shipping form (download and open the PDF locally). Mail to: Artist's Book Application, Women's Studio Workshop, PO Box 489, Rosendale, NY 12472. Applications open 1 August 2026; deadline 15 November 2026, 11:59pm EST; notification March 2027; residency runs September through June, one year after application. Apply via Submittable at https://wsworkshop.org/residencies/artists-book-residency-grant/.

grants.title: Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship 2026
grants.location: Ottawa, Canada; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada.
grants.amount: Up to $5,000 (including expenses and/or stipend). No application fee.
grants.description: The National Gallery of Canada's Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship encourages and supports advanced research in Canadian and Indigenous Canadian war and military art. Research must relate to any aspect of Canadian war or military art (including painting, drawing, sculpture, photography, printmaking, and other media) and must emphasise the use and investigation of the collections of the National Gallery of Canada, the Canadian War Museum, and Library and Archives Canada. The Fellowship supports original research by scholars undertaking publishing, exhibition, thesis, and dissertation work or their equivalents. AMOUNT: up to $5,000, including expenses and/or stipend. TENURE: one calendar year beginning 1 September 2026; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada. ELIGIBILITY: open to art historians, curators, critics, conservators, graduate students, and independent or other professionals working in the visual arts, museology, and related disciplines in the humanities and social sciences; international applicants welcome. Researchers from outside Canada must ensure all visa requirements and documentation are in order before entry and the start of the Fellowship. Applications are invited from all qualified candidates, including Indigenous peoples, women, people of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression, racialized people, and people with disabilities. TO APPLY: submit a complete application in English or French as a single PDF by email to Amy Rose, Senior Manager, Library, Archives and Research Fellowship Program, at erefel@gallery.ca. Deadline: 26 June 2026.

grants.title: GovAI UK Winter Fellowship 2027 - Research Track
grants.organization: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
grants.location: London, UK; in-person, full-time.
grants.amount: £12,000 salary for the three-month term, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch provided. Visa sponsorship (3-month temporary work visa) available. No application fee.
grants.description: GovAI's UK Winter Fellowship (Research Track) is a three-month in-person fellowship in London for researchers and policy professionals seeking to enter or advance careers in AI governance and policy. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice, paired with a GovAI supervisor from the team or network; the first two weeks are spent exploring the AI governance landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Research outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI governance. Alongside the project, fellows attend expert Q&As, weekly seminars, skills workshops, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities with GovAI's team and network. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US, UK, and EU government; leading AI companies (Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI); top think tanks (CSET, RAND); and leading universities (Oxford, Cambridge). AMOUNT: £12,000 salary for three months, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch. Visa sponsorship for a 3-month temporary work visa is available; successful visa applicants must remain in their country of application while the visa is processed. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); no strict degree requirements; relevant areas include public policy, political science, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. Senior candidates who need accommodations should email fellowships@governance.ai with subject line 'senior candidate winter fellowship 2027' and a CV. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement in prioritising research directions, and team fit (intellectual honesty, openness to feedback, comfort with uncertainty). APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Deadline: 12 July 2026, 23:59 BST.

grants.title: GovAI DC Winter Fellowship 2027
grants.organization: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
grants.location: Washington, DC, USA; in-person, full-time (part-time possible for DC-based think tank or government staff).
grants.amount: $21,000 stipend for the three-month term, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch provided. No visa sponsorship. No application fee.
grants.description: GovAI's DC Winter Fellowship is a three-month bipartisan in-person fellowship in Washington, DC, designed for individuals seeking to enter or advance in US AI governance and policy. The program emphasises bipartisan engagement, rigorous analysis, and practical policy relevance, with a focus on how AI policy is shaped within the US political system. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice with mentorship from a GovAI supervisor; the first two weeks explore the AI policy landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI policy. Fellows also attend expert Q&As, skills workshops, seminars, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US government bodies (NSC, OSTP, Senate, Department of Commerce, DHS); think tanks (CSIS, CSET, CNAS, RAND, Heritage Foundation, FAI); AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind); and universities (Yale, Stanford, MIT). AMOUNT: $21,000 stipend for three months, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch. Part-time fellowship at a pro-rated salary available for DC-based think tank researchers, government staffers, or professionals doing AI governance work; pay accommodations possible. ELIGIBILITY: candidates must have the right to work in the US; no visa sponsorship. Open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); promising undergraduates welcome; no strict degree requirements. Relevant areas include public policy, political science, engineering, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement, and team fit. APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Application deadline not yet publicly stated; applications open June 2026 -- check programme page for the deadline.

grants.title: microagi Research Fellowship - Cohort 01
grants.location: Munich, Germany or Zurich, Switzerland; in-person, 6-month residence.
grants.amount: Up to $2,000,000 in compute resources; $10,000/month stipend. No application fee stated. $10,000 referral bonus if you refer an accepted fellow.
grants.description: The microagi Research Fellowship (Cohort 01) provides researchers with frontier-lab resources and the freedom to work on hard problems in robotics, embodied AI, and the systems around them. Fellows work in residence for six months at microagi's offices in Munich or Zurich. AMOUNT: up to $2,000,000 in compute; $10,000/month stipend. RESEARCH AREAS: (1) Long Horizon Dexterous Manipulation -- end-to-end models for complex multi-stage tasks with dexterous hands, natural language instruction following, memory across long task sequences, fine-grained physical control in unstructured environments; (2) Cross Embodiment Learning; (3) On Device Physical Intelligence; (4) Safe and Reliable Deployment. microagi's model is to deploy embodied intelligence into physical-world operations and turn what it learns there into compounding research. REFERRAL: $10,000 bonus for referring a candidate who is accepted into the cohort. No application deadline publicly stated as of June 2026 -- one cohort only, apply as soon as possible via https://www.microagi.ai/fellowship.

grants.title: Claude Corps Fellowship
grants.organization: Anthropic (funded); CodePath (employer of record); Social Finance (evaluation)
grants.location: USA; in-person at host nonprofit organizations across America (placement location varies; relocation support available).
grants.amount: $85,000 full-time annual salary plus benefits, expansive Claude token budget, and relocation support as needed. No application fee.
grants.description: Claude Corps is a national fellowship program from Anthropic placing early-career fellows full-time at nonprofits across America for one year, with the goal of extending AI's benefits to communities and building fellows' AI skills. Anthropic funds the program and provides Claude expertise; CodePath (America's largest collegiate CS education provider) is the fellows' official employer of record and leads programming; Social Finance leads measurement and evaluation. AMOUNT: $85,000 full-time salary plus benefits; expansive Claude token budget; relocation support as needed. FELLOW EXPERIENCE: intensive Claude training at the start; five hours of ongoing weekly training throughout; support from a CodePath mentor; Anthropic office hours for technical questions; and professional guidance from the host organization manager. SAMPLE HOST ORGANIZATIONS: Braven (Chicago), Code the Dream (Durham NC), Heartland Forward (Bentonville AR), Montgomery County Food Bank (Conroe TX), Reef Environmental Education Foundation (Key Largo FL), StriveTogether (Cincinnati), YMCA of Greater Charlotte, and more than 400 nonprofits total. ELIGIBILITY: anyone over 18 with under two years of full-time work experience, regardless of educational background; must be authorized to work in the US; must be comfortable working with Claude and willing to relocate if necessary. COHORTS: Cohort 1 (100 fellows, starts October 2026) -- applications close 17 July 2026; Cohort 2 (starts January 2027) and Cohort 3 (starts August 2027) -- rolling applications. Apply at https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-corps.

grants.title: SOUND/IMAGE 2026: "Artistic Intelligence" - Call for Works
grants.location: University of Greenwich, London, UK; in-person attendance required.
grants.amount: No payment to artists. All accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25 (covers catering and technical support).
grants.description: SOUND/IMAGE 2026 is the 11th edition of the University of Greenwich festival exploring sound and image practice, running 5-8 November 2026 in London. This year's theme is "Artistic Intelligence": a critical reflection on creative choice, agency, and authenticity in the context of algorithmic and AI-mediated art making. The festival cites reference points from Vera Molnár, Pauline Oliveros, John Cage, Iannis Xenakis, Ryoji Ikeda, and Refik Anadol. Special consideration is given to students, apprentices, and emerging artists; submissions are welcome from composers, filmmakers, artists, performers, and architects. CATEGORIES ACCEPTED: sound and music works; audiovisual works; talks; artist statements; workshops and practical demonstrations; live performances; installations. THEMATIC AREAS: embodied knowledge and lived experience in aesthetic judgement; creative choice vs chance encounters; ownership, authenticity, and AI co-creation; human and algorithmic capability; spatial and immersive audio-visual forms; interdisciplinary practice (CreaTech); practice research. SUBMISSION RULES: works under 15 minutes in duration (installations exempt from duration limit); maximum three submissions per artist across all categories; artists must attend and present in person; submit separately for each submission via Microsoft CMT. FESTIVAL PASS: all accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25. Notifications before end of July 2026. Deadline: 19 June 2026, 23:59 UTC+1 (extended from 12 June). Submit at https://blogs.gre.ac.uk/sound-image/sound-image-2026-call-for-works/.

grants.title: Revitalizing Rituals 2026 Open Call
grants.location: Bhar Lazreg, Tunisia and Somogy County, Hungary; multi-stage in-person residency across both sites.
grants.amount: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. No application fee.
grants.duration: Multi-stage residency: Placemaking Camp (1 week, November 2026, Hungary); Storytellers Camp (1 week, March 2027, Tunisia); Residency Period (minimum 1 month, April-July 2027); public presentation (August 2027); Closing Event (3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary).
grants.description: Revitalizing Rituals invites artists from all disciplines -- sound, movement, visual arts, installation, performance, and transdisciplinary practices -- to join a research-creation journey across two landscapes: Bhar Lazreg in Tunisia and the wetlands of Somogy County in Hungary. The project explores rituals as living forms of belonging: reconnecting communities, land, memory, food, ecological reciprocity, and the more-than-human world. Selected artists work with local communities as co-participants in a shared inquiry into moving from extraction toward reciprocity. Organised by Homo Faber Alapítvány (Hungary). AMOUNT: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. ELIGIBILITY: minimum 5 years of active artistic practice; portfolio reflecting interest in situated or relational work; no formal degree required; working language is English (local language navigation valued); must be available for the full residency cycle including all international travel; must be able to legally receive the fee and invoice the organising partners from country of residence. Strongly encourages applications from historically marginalised or underrepresented voices. KEY DATES: Application Deadline 19 July 2026 (midnight) -- note the open call PDF also references a 5 July date; the officially promoted deadline is 19 July; Online Q&A 29 June 2026, 14:00 Tunisia / 15:00 CET (register at https://forms.gle/brnZvXvFe4JB8zL26); Online Conversations with shortlisted artists: mid July to mid August 2026; Selection Results: 31 August 2026; Placemaking Camp: 1 week, end of November 2026, Hungary; Storytellers Camp: 1 week, March 2027, Tunisia; Residency Period: minimum 1 month (splittable), April to July 2027; Final Presentation: August 2027; Closing Event: 3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary. Apply via the open call form linked at https://homofaber.hu/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Artist-Open-Call-FINAL-in-the-RR-form.pdf.

grants.title: Hellmouth Magazine: Volume 3 "Metamorphosis" - Call for Submissions
grants.location: Knoxville, TN, USA; Southeast US focus for reviews, but national and international exceptions considered. Remote submissions.
grants.amount: $50 honorarium upon publication. No submission fee.
grants.description: Hellmouth Magazine, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit arts and culture publication based in Knoxville, TN, is seeking submissions for Volume 3 on the theme of "Metamorphosis." The magazine publishes twice annually, covering experimental essays, critical art writing, reviews, interviews, and visual art. HONORARIUM: $50 upon publication. CATEGORIES OPEN: Reviews (exhibitions, gallery events, artist shows, cultural happenings -- Southeast focus but national/international exceptions welcomed; 500-750 words); Interviews (artists living or dead; video interviews also welcome; open word count); Art Writing (academic or experimental essays on contemporary or historical art -- artists, movements, zeitgeist, concepts, theories; 1,200+ words); Visual Art (digital output or print; best submitted alongside an upcoming show or event). FICTION & POETRY: closed for this volume. THEME: Metamorphosis as transformative vehicle -- post-Anthropocentric systems, chimeras, the post-human and transhuman, cultural and political shapeshifting, transformation through art and literature in the wake of collective trauma. All pitches should be approximately 500 words. Read recent issues before submitting. Submissions due 20 June 2026. Submit at https://www.hellmouthmag.com/submit.

grants.title: Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships - Class of 2027
grants.organization: Samstag Museum of Art, University of South Australia
grants.location: Australia (applicants must be Australian citizens or permanent residents); 12-month placement at an international institution of the scholar's choice.
grants.amount: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year of study (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. No application fee.
grants.duration: 12 months overseas at an international learning institution.
grants.description: The Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships are annual scholarships enabling Australian artists to develop their artistic capacities through a dedicated year of practice-based learning at an international institution of their choice. AMOUNT: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. ELIGIBILITY: Australian citizens or permanent residents aged 18 or over who are either (a) currently enrolled in a practice-led visual arts program at an Australian institution of higher education, having successfully completed at least two years of an undergraduate or postgraduate degree; or (b) graduates of a practice-led visual arts undergraduate or postgraduate qualification at an Australian institution of higher education (graduates of more than five years' standing are now eligible following 2023 guideline updates). PROCESS: applicants must create an account in the online portal, download the Samstag Microsoft PowerPoint template, read the Class of 2027 Guidelines, and complete and submit the online application form. Accounts can be created before the deadline and applications saved for later submission. Prior applicants (Classes of 2024, 2025, or 2026) can reuse the same account. Deadline: 30 June 2026, midnight Australian Central Standard Time (ACST). Apply at https://samstag.adelaide.edu.au/scholarship/apply/.

grants.title: Small File Media Festival 2026 - Call for Work
grants.location: Canada; festival screening. Open to international submissions.
grants.amount: No payment stated; festival programming and screening. No submission fee stated.
grants.description: The Small File Media Festival 2026 invites moving-image submissions under the theme "Can Small Files Save the World?" -- a call for arthouse, video work, glitch aesthetics, punk, sci-fi, narrative, and documentary work made with a small-file mindset. This year the festival especially welcomes small-scale experiments with narrative and documentary. The festival frames Small File Ecomedia as a political and ecological intervention: small files function in regions with precarious infrastructure, circumvent bandwidth imperialism and surveillance, and expose the environmental cost of mainstream online video. WHAT THEY WANT: craftily composed, elegantly performed, and artfully minimised movies in any genre; use of obsolete technologies, creative compression techniques, and low-bandwidth aesthetics at all stages of production is encouraged. Resources for making small-file work at www.smallfile.ca/make. Open to artists, activists, media pirates, rogue technologists, and filmmakers. No payment or submission fee stated. All submitted information used for festival programming and promotion -- ensure accurate spelling, punctuation, and capitalisation. Submissions close 1 July 2026 at midnight. Submit at https://smallfile.ca/submit/.

grants.title: Híbrides: Small Embodied Data - Call for Contributions (2nd Edition)
grants.organization: Axolot.cat collective and DARTS research group, Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC)
grants.location: Barcelona, Spain; UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí. In-person attendance required.
grants.amount: Oral presentations: €100 honorarium (invoice required). Performances: €400 honorarium (invoice required). No submission fee.
grants.description: Híbrides is a biennial of artistic research co-organised by the Axolot.cat collective and the DARTS (Design, Art, Technology and Society) research group at the UOC in Barcelona. The second edition, "Small Embodied Data," centres on the concept of classification -- approached through machine learning, data science, algorithmic systems, and questions of who designs classifiers, under what ethical codes, and to what end. The biennial brings classification into dialogue with its previous themes of Small Data (community-defined, intentional data collection and analysis) and Embodiment (bodily experience as a foundation of situated, sensory knowledge). Both academic and non-academic contributions are welcome. FORMATS ACCEPTED: Oral Presentations (20 minutes; artistic practice, artistic research, or academic research projects; may be video-recorded for a public archive and developed into short papers); Performances (20-30 minutes; must engage with the physical body, stage, and technology; complete works, including previously presented work; takes place in a multipurpose space with technical support). SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS: online form with abstract (max 1,000 characters), professional biography (max 1,000 characters), and link to representative materials (3-5 images; video and technical rider required for performances). HONORARIA: oral presentations €100; performances €400; invoice required in both cases. Participants must follow the event's code of conduct. Event runs 26-28 November 2026 at UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí, Barcelona. Deadline: 7 September 2026. Submit at https://hibrides.axolot.cat/opencall.html.

grants.title: BMI Foundation Babyface Award 2026
grants.location: USA; remote application.
grants.amount: $10,000 career grant plus artistic and business mentorship. No application fee.
grants.description: The BMI Foundation Babyface Award is an inaugural annual award supporting emerging songwriters working in R&B, hip-hop, soul, and music of the African diaspora. Founded with a $25,000 investment from Wells Fargo. AMOUNT: $10,000 career grant plus artistic and business mentorship. ELIGIBILITY: ages 18-29 as of 30 June 2026; US residents or those in US territories; submitted works must be unpublished and unassigned to major labels or publishers (self-released works are eligible); co-written works are eligible if all writers meet the eligibility criteria (maximum 3 writers; award split evenly). One application per person per year; cannot submit the same work to multiple BMI Foundation programs. No affiliation with BMI required. APPLICATION MATERIALS: a 1-2 minute personal statement video; two audio files with anonymized lyric sheets (lyrics may be in any language with English translation); and a description of the creative process (audio, visual, or written format). No application fee. Apply via Google Form at https://bmifoundation.org/by-application/babyfaceaward. Deadline: 30 June 2026.

grants.title: The Wheeler Centre: The Next Chapter 2027
grants.location: Melbourne, Australia; includes residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House, and an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre.
grants.amount: AUD $15,000 stipend per fellow. No application fee.
grants.description: The Next Chapter is The Wheeler Centre's flagship writing program, designed to elevate Australian stories that are underrepresented and to nurture a new wave of writers to tell them. Each fellow receives a AUD $15,000 stipend and is paired with a handpicked mentor for a twelve-month period to progress a major writing project. Fellows also receive a residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House; an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre; and meaningful opportunities to connect with peers, agents, publishers, and readers. One fellowship place is dedicated specifically to a poet: The Malcolm Robertson Fellowship. 2027 judges: Melissa Lucashenko, Micaela Sahhar, Cher Tan, and Melanie Saward. ELIGIBILITY: Australian writers. No specific genre restrictions stated; the program focuses on stories that are not currently being told and voices that are underrepresented. TIMELINE: submissions open 2 June 2026; submissions close 31 July 2026 (5pm); 2027 Next Chapter Fellows announced 10 November 2026. Questions: thenextchapter@wheelercentre.com. Apply at https://www.wheelercentre.com/the-next-chapter.

grants.title: The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers 2026
grants.organization: Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia
grants.location: Australia; must currently reside in Australia.
grants.amount: AUD $10,000 prize money (donated by Hachette Australia); 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher; first option to consider finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. No application fee.
grants.description: The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers, presented by the Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia, is an annual prize for unpublished writers of adult fiction and adult narrative non-fiction. The winner receives AUD $10,000 and a 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher, who will work with the writer to develop their manuscript, with first option to consider the finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers are also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. The prize was established in memory of Hachette Australia CEO Matt Richell (d. 2014). ELIGIBILITY: must currently reside in Australia (Australian citizens, permanent residents, and long-term visa holders residing in Australia; those residing overseas are not eligible); unpublished writers only -- no previous publication by a publishing house (including small press or independent publishers in any genre); publication in anthologies, literary journals, scholarly works, writing PhD projects, or self-publishing does not disqualify. ELIGIBLE GENRES: adult fiction (including short story collections) and adult narrative non-fiction (including memoir and essay collections). Poetry, scripts, plays, picture books, practical non-fiction, and works for children or young adults are not eligible. SUBMISSION FORMAT: single PDF comprising the first three chapters (maximum 20,000 words), a one-page synopsis, and a chapter breakdown of up to two A4 pages; plus a statement (up to 750 words) on how winning the prize will further your writing career, entered in the online form. Manuscript must be complete or intended to be completed; does not need to be finished at time of submission. A4, double-spaced, 12pt Roman font (e.g. Garamond or Times New Roman), 3cm margins, page numbers. TIMELINE: prize opened 23 April 2026; closes 13 July 2026 at 11:59pm AEST; longlist announced 11 September 2026; shortlist 16 October 2026; winner announced 6 November 2026. Administered with support from Simpsons Solicitors. Apply at https://emergingwritersfestival.org.au/richell-prize-2026/.

grants.title: AdK / EMS Berlin-Stockholm Bilateral Residency Program 2026-27
grants.organization: Studio für Elektroakustische Musik, Akademie der Künste (Berlin) and Elektronmusikstudion EMS (Stockholm)
grants.location: Berlin, Germany and Stockholm, Sweden; travel and accommodation covered for stays in both cities.
grants.amount: Travel and accommodation costs for stays in Berlin and Stockholm covered; fees paid for presentation at KONTAKTE Festival 2027 (Berlin) and for presentation in Stockholm. Amount not publicly stated. No application fee.
grants.duration: Flexible, by agreement; works to be presented at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a Stockholm presentation.
grants.description: A new bilateral residency collaboration between the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik at the Akademie der Künste in Berlin and Elektronmusikstudion EMS in Stockholm. Four artists will be selected to develop and present new works in both cities, with presentations at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a corresponding event in Stockholm. The program supports new artistic and technical initiatives at the intersection of sound, music, and technology in the field of time-based, performative, and digital arts. ELIGIBLE CATEGORIES: (1) new instrumental and live-electronic works; (2) acousmatic music, computer music, radio art, and other forms of electroacoustic music and sound art; (3) projects from artists of any discipline for whom sound is a central element; (4) curatorial, publishing, and educational projects in sound-based art practice, particularly those relating in content to the AdK Studio or EMS; (5) independent research in acoustics, computer science, or musicology -- specifically software development, spatialization, or network technologies. Open to individuals and groups. Project duration is flexible and arranged by agreement. SUPPORT: travel and accommodation costs for Berlin and Stockholm covered; presentation fees paid for both venues. Selection is made by the director and advisory board of the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik and the director of EMS; decision is final with no legal entitlement to funding. Incomplete or late applications are not considered. This call supplements the Studio's regular residency program (separate deadline June 30, 2026 for July-December 2026 period). Apply exclusively via the online form at https://nc.adk.de/index.php/apps/forms/s/HD2nxbzqwgeMHWN6xHg9bQrA. Questions: estudio@adk.de. Deadline: 31 July 2026.

grants.title: Democracy Stories Lab: Open Call for Visual Storytellers
grants.organization: People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance
grants.location: Worldwide; remote. Global screening event for winners.
grants.amount: USD $1,000 for each of 4 winners; USD $500 for each of 5 honorable mentions. Plus amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance networks and feature in a global screening event. No application fee.
grants.description: The Democracy Stories Lab, an initiative of People Powered (a global hub for participatory democracy) in partnership with the Democracy Narratives Alliance, invites creators worldwide to submit visual content -- short videos, illustrations, or graphics -- that tells a compelling story about democracy, voice, fairness, belonging, and community power. Content can be original or already published, as long as it is the applicant's own work and engages with narrative frameworks from the Democracy Narratives Alliance's research on 'How to Talk About Democracy.' Tone is open; serious and comedic approaches are both welcome. AWARDS: 4 winning entries receive USD $1,000 each; 5 honorable mentions receive USD $500 each; all winners receive amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance communications networks and are featured in a global screening event. WHO CAN APPLY: individual creators and small teams anywhere in the world; especially welcomes creators who reach audiences outside usual democracy circles, who come from communities traditionally with less of a say in public discourse, and who work in civic tech, advocacy, community organising, comedy, art, illustration, games, or lifestyle content. Applications open 14 July 2026 via the Democracy Stories Lab page at https://www.peoplepowered.org/news-content/announcing-the-democracy-stories-lab; subscribe to the People Powered newsletter to be notified when the form opens. Submission deadline: 14 September 2026. Winners announced October-November 2026. Note: the initiative was itself voted into existence through People Powered's participatory budgeting process (PP Decides).

grants.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
grants.location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.
grants.amount: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.
grants.duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.
grants.description: NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

grants.title: Center for Humane Technology: Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship (Inaugural Cohort)
grants.location: Remote, 10 hours/week overlapping with EST required.
grants.amount: $30,000 fixed stipend paid in three milestone-based tranches. No application fee.
grants.duration: 6 months: September 14, 2026 through March 18, 2027. 15 hours per week.
grants.description: CHT's inaugural Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship places 3 fellows (one per focus area) in a 6-month, part-time program to develop rigorous public-impact research and writing on how AI is reshaping society. Fellows choose from 5 focus areas: (1) AI and Cognition — what AI does to human judgment, critical thinking, and intellectual independence; (2) AI and Relationships — AI's effects on intimacy, companionship, and social bonds; (3) AI and Surveillance — how AI enables monitoring, tracking, and control; (4) AI and Spirituality — AI's intersection with meaning, transcendence, and religious practice; (5) Inside the Machine: AI From a Technologist's Perspective. Up to 2 focus areas per application. DELIVERABLES: one flagship research piece, report, or long-form essay for public impact; 1-3 op-eds (placed in target outlets or CHT Substack); one podcast appearance (Your Undivided Attention or equivalent); cohort meetings and bi-weekly check-ins. SUPPORT: bi-weekly guidance from Senior Director of Strategy and Impact; editorial, communications and graphic design support; media training and PR placement support; CHT email address and Slack access; listing on CHT's public fellows page. WHO SHOULD APPLY: mid-career researchers (psychology, sociology, philosophy, cognitive science, communications, STS), policy analysts, journalists covering tech and society, civil society advocates, or technologists with insider perspective. Strong preference for those with a public writing track record (op-eds, Substack, peer-reviewed work) and some public presence. HOW TO APPLY: 1-2 page research proposal per topic (up to 2), CV/resume (2 pages max), 2-minute video explaining fit, writing sample. Deadline: Sunday 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

grants.title: Horizon Europe Cluster 2: Culture, Creativity and Inclusive Society (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE) — incl. AI and Creative Content topic
grants.organization: European Research Executive Agency (REA) / European Commission, Horizon Europe
grants.location: European Union and associated countries. Consortium-based; typically requires partners from multiple EU/Horizon member states.
grants.amount: Indicative total budget €85.5 million across 7 topics (2026 call). Individual project amounts vary by topic. Up to 100% funding rate for non-profit research organizations. No application fee.
grants.description: Horizon Europe Cluster 2 funds research on culture, creativity, and inclusive society. The 2026 call (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE, launched May 12, 2026) covers 7 topics with an indicative budget of €85.5 million. Topic HERITAGE-04 is explicitly titled 'Towards a fair and transparent market for cultural and creative content in the era of generative AI,' covering: impacts of generative AI on creative industries employment; upskilling needs for creative workers; policy and regulatory frameworks; fair remuneration and transparency. Highly relevant for research into AI and cultural/creative industries, artists' rights in the AI era, and sociotechnical studies of AI and culture. ELIGIBILITY: consortium-based applications (minimum 3 organisations from 3 different EU/associated countries are typical for Horizon RIA grants; check call conditions on the Funding & Tenders portal for specific requirements). Deadline: 23 September 2026. Apply via the EU Funding & Tenders portal (ec.europa.eu/info/funding-tenders).

grants.title: Omidyar Network: Tech Journalism Fund
grants.location: Open to journalists and media organizations worldwide.
grants.amount: $5,000-$25,000 per project. Rolling deadline through December 31, 2026. No application fee.
grants.duration: Per-project; scope and timeline defined in the proposal.
grants.description: The Omidyar Network Tech Journalism Fund offers rolling grants of $5,000-$25,000 for technology journalism projects. Relevant for journalists and media organizations investigating AI, tech policy, and AI's impacts on culture, society, and creative industries. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through December 31, 2026 — one of the few currently open opportunities with a confirmed near-term deadline. Apply via the application form on omidyar.com.

grants.title: European Commission: Safeguarding Europe's Born-Digital Heritage (PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL)
grants.organization: European Commission (Pilot Projects and Preparatory Actions, Culture strand)
grants.location: European Union. Consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU member states required.
grants.amount: Up to €1,985,000, covering up to 85% of eligible project costs (15% co-financing required). No application fee.
grants.duration: Project duration to be defined in the proposal; covers a scoping and mapping phase across EU member states.
grants.description: The European Commission is offering a single grant of up to €1,985,000 (85% co-financing) for a project focused on safeguarding born-digital cultural heritage across EU member states. Scope includes: assessing the preservation state of born-digital art, web archives, video games, and digital content created outside cultural institutions; mapping EU and national legal frameworks; identifying non-legislative barriers (technical, financial, organisational, cultural); and providing guidance on creating preservation copies of licensed or legally accessible materials. This is a scoping and advocacy project, not a digitisation grant. ELIGIBILITY: the application must be led by a consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU countries, which rules out solo applicants and single-country organisations. Grant reference: PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL. Deadline: 16 July 2026. Verify the current deadline at ec.europa.eu before applying (one secondary source listed 1 June 2026; the primary call-fiche PDF and the Cyprus government funding portal both confirm 16 July 2026).

grants.title: NEH: Collections Stewardship (December 2026 cycle)
grants.location: United States. Applicants must be US-based institutions.
grants.amount: Up to $350,000 for single organizations; up to $500,000 for consortia. Projects up to 3 years. No application fee.
grants.description: NEH Collections Stewardship funds preservation projects at libraries, archives, museums, colleges/universities, and preservation-services organisations. Eligible activities include digitisation and reformatting of collections for public access through digital repositories, preservation planning, and environmental assessments. Relevant for organisations working with born-digital or digitally reformatted collections including software, AI artefacts, and digital cultural heritage. Applications for the December 2026 cycle open 31 August 2026; deadline 15 December 2026. A May 2026 cycle has already passed. ELIGIBILITY: institutional applicants only (libraries, archives, museums, colleges, universities, preservation-services organisations based in the US); individual researchers and for-profit entities are not eligible. Apply via grants.gov.

grants.title: Freelands Foundation: Learning Through Making Fund
grants.location: UK (England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, Wales); activity must occur within the United Kingdom.
grants.amount: Up to £25,000. No application fee.
grants.duration: Projects of any length, taking place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. Funding provided no earlier than December 2026.
